The lake bottom has limited sandy areas near the western lobe, with the rest covered in peat mud. Fish species in the lake include perch, pike, roach, and bream.

locations.title: Jussi Pikkjärv
locations.description: Jussi Pikkjärv, nestled between two ridges, has an elongated shape in the north-south direction. With a surface area of 6.1 hectares, a maximum depth of 9.7 meters, and an average depth of 3.5 meters, the lake's shores are steep and wooded on the east and west sides. Connected to Kõverjärv through a ditch, the lake is stratified, exhibiting a greenish-yellow color with moderate transparency (2.5 meters). In 1943, perch dominated the lake, with occasional silver bream and roach, and silver bream were introduced.

locations.title: Jussi Suurjärv
locations.description: Jussi Suurjärv is the largest among the Jussi lakes, covering an area of 18.6 hectares, with an average depth of 2.7 meters and a maximum depth of 5.7 meters. The lake is inhabited by perch, roach, pike, bream, and occasionally, tench.

locations.title: Jussi Väinjärv
locations.description: Jussi Väinjärv, the northernmost lake in the Jussi lake group, is situated east of the ridge. It is the shallowest in the group, reaching a depth of 4.2 meters (average depth 2.6 meters). The western shore, facing the ridge, is high and wooded, while the rest of the shoreline is low, marshy, covered with meadows and bushes. The lake bottom is predominantly muddy. The primary fish species in the lake are roach and perch, with fewer occurrences of pike, cod, and tench.

locations.title: Jussi Mustjärv
locations.description: Jussi Mustjärv is a small, three-lobed lake surrounded by ridges and heavily marshy areas, with a relatively narrow strip of bog encircling it. Despite its size (2.1 hectares), the lake is quite deep, reaching 9.5 meters with an average depth of 4.2 meters. The lake has a muddy bottom and is characterized as a dystrophic lake. It is very poor in aquatic vegetation, and there is no information available about the fish population in the lake.

locations.title: Sõõriksoo
locations.description: "Sõõriksoo," formerly the Aegviidu-side section of Rehessaare bog, was known for its peat industry in the early 20th century, at that time being Estonia's largest (contributing nearly 50% of the country's peat production). Remnants of the peat extraction, such as peat ditches interspersed with higher ridges and haulage roads, are still clearly visible in the natural landscape. Over time, the bog vegetation has largely recovered, with the presence of cranberries, heather, cottongrass, white beak-rush, and other characteristic bog plants.

locations.title: Paukjärv
locations.description: Paukjärve, with an area of 8.6 hectares and a maximum depth of 11.1 meters (average depth 5.9 meters), is characterized by a rapid deepening of its water. The lake is surrounded by hills covered in light-rich pine forests, except for the southwestern shore, which is lower. The shore is mostly firm, sandy, and the mineral soil descends directly into the water. Remains of a swimming area and building foundations on the northwestern shore indicate the lake's past use as a recreational spot. Paukjärve is a dystrophic lake, seemingly nourished mainly by rainfall. The lake has exceptionally poor vegetation, with perch dominating the fish population, along with roach and pike. Silver bream has also been introduced into the lake.

locations.title: Metstoa Linajärv
locations.description: Metstoa Linajärv is a small natural lake located along the Paukjärve hiking trail. With a water surface area of 0.9 hectares and a shoreline length of 368 meters, it provides a scenic spot for hikers to enjoy the natural surroundings.

locations.title: Ümarjärv
locations.description: Metstoa Ümarjärv is situated along the Paukjärve hiking trail, featuring a water surface area of 1.7 hectares and a shoreline length of 493 meters. It is characterized by soft and clear water

locations.title: Kaasikjärv
locations.description: Kaasikjärv is the furthest point on the Paukjärve hiking trail. This natural lake has a surface area of 0.7 hectares, with a shoreline length of 317 meters, providing a serene destination for hikers on the trail.

locations.title: Elu Kivil
locations.description: "Elu kivil" is a large glacial erratic from the Ice Age, marking the beginning of the Majakivi hiking trail.

locations.title: Aabla Bog
locations.description: Aabla bog, also known as Keitme or Keitnõmme swamp, is one of the larger marshes on the Northern Estonian coastal plain. The formation of the swamp began over 7000 years ago when rising land levels trapped seawater between the Pikanõmme ridge and the coastal ridges emerging from the east, forming a lagoon. Over time, the waterlogged area turned into a marsh and gradually became overgrown.

locations.title: Pikanõmme Ridge
locations.description: Pikanõmme ridge is an almost 4 km long, crescent-shaped chain of former coastal formations, with heights reaching up to 10 meters above the base. The highest point on Pikanõmme ridge, at the tower, is also the highest point on the entire peninsula, rising 33 meters above sea level. Behind the ridge, a gradually rising swamp has developed, now standing 3 meters higher than the surrounding areas. In the lower areas of Pikanõmme, especially at its southern end, one can observe how the swamp is slowly burying its creators – the sand dunes.

locations.title: Aabla Transitional Bog
locations.description: This bog represents a transitional bog, the most common type in the central part of the Juminda Peninsula. The transitional bog pine forests spread in narrow and wider strips along the edges of the swamp and in overgrown depressions between former coastal formations. When the peat surface of the fen rises so high that it is no longer flooded by high water levels, and bog plants start to grow, a transitional bog is formed. Transitional bogs serve as habitats and feeding grounds for various forest animals and birds. Wild boar paths run through here, elk feed on the vegetation, and bears indulge in berries. During the nesting and chick-rearing season, it's nearly impossible to notice common cranes, but in the fall, the forest and swamp echo with their calls.

locations.title: Rõõsa Lake
locations.description: Rõõsa Lake is surrounded by forested hills to the south and northwest, while the shores are lower but relatively firm elsewhere. Most of the lake is bordered by woods, with only the side facing the road featuring Rõõsa forest ranger's field and meadow. The lake is not very deep (4.5 meters, with only 3-3.5 meters of clear water), but it deepens sharply near the shore. As the lake freezes over in winter, the fish population is noticeably impoverished, with only small and lean silver bream being abundant.

locations.title: Suur Kaksjärv
locations.description: Suur Kaksjärv, located barely 50 meters west of Väike Kaksjärv, is an oval lake spanning 2.2 hectares with a depth of 6.2 meters. It seems that Suur and Väike Kaksjärv were once part of a single water body. On the northern side, where the terrain rises, there is a spruce-mixed forest, while the rest is covered by a boggy pine forest. The lake has minimal throughflow. Suur Kaksjärv is known for having some of the redder water in Estonia, with a brownish-red or reddish-brown hue and low transparency (0.7-1.5 meters). The water is strongly stratified. In late winter and spring, rusty "blooms" appear on the water's surface, causing harm to fish. The main fish species is perch, with occasional presence of silver bream, pike, and roach. Silver bream has been introduced into the lake.

locations.title: Lindjärv
locations.description: Lindjärv is a 7-hectare lake with a depth of 1.5 meters. The water has a reddish-brown color, and transparency is low, ranging from 0.7 to 1.0 meters. The main fish species in the lake is the tench (koger), and there are also reports of pike, perch, roach, and, according to some data, even bream.

locations.title: Rahkjärv
locations.description: Rahkjärv is located 400 meters southwest of Suur Kaksjärv. With a surface area of 4.0 hectares, a maximum depth of 2.8 meters (average depth 1.9 meters), the lake is situated just north of the scenic Teedelahkme ridge in the bog. Steep peat shores descend sharply into the water, and the lakebed is covered with mud containing numerous empty shells of snails, including lake snail shells over 10 cm in length. The lake has weak throughflow. The water color varies from yellow to reddish-brown, with low transparency (0.5-1.8 meters), and it is mixed and warms up. The fish population includes roach, pike, perch, tench, and silver bream.

locations.title: Mustjärv
locations.description: Paunküla Mustjärv is surrounded by a bog pine forest, and the shoreline is bordered by peat moss. The lakebed is covered with peat mud. There is very weak throughflow, and the lake water is reddish-brown, with low transparency (0.9-1.2 meters) and strong stratification. Rusty "blooms" appear on the water's surface in late winter, and the lake freezes over in winter. Tarn grows in some places along the shoreline and extends into the water with peat moss. There are no other aquatic plants in the lake. The fish population mainly consists of perch, with reports of pike, roach, silver bream, and tench.

locations.title: Punamäe Lake
locations.description: Punamäe Lake is surrounded by a moist mixed forest, and there is a considerable amount of reed-covered shoreline. The only areas without reeds are the northern shore and the central part of the southern shore. The lakebed is covered with a layer of mud. There is very weak throughflow, and the water color is reddish-brown or orange. Transparency is low to moderate (0.7-2.1 meters). The fish population includes plenty of roaches and perch, as well as pike, roach, and tench. The latter two can grow quite large, with individuals weighing up to a kilogram.

locations.title: Keila River Crossing
locations.description: Keila-Joa Crossing is a bridge in the middle of the Keila-Joa Nature trail that provides a good view over the river and surrounding scenery.

locations.title: Meremõisa Ruins
locations.description: Meremõisa Manor was founded in the 1580s. It is located approximately 500 meters west of Keila-Joa Manor, on the opposite (left) bank of the river. At that time, Meremõisa became a subsidiary manor of Keila-Joa, although no new buildings were constructed there, except for the remains. Some ruins still exist today, though in much smaller quantities compared to the 19th century and the early 20th century.

locations.title: Keila-Joa Park
locations.description: Keila-Joa Park's terrain is diverse, featuring high dunes covered with coniferous and deciduous trees, interspersed with picturesque valleys, meadows, and cozy glades. The axis of the park is defined by the Keila River, and the park's territory is home to nearly 200 streams.

locations.title: Valgejärv
locations.description: Riisipere Valgejärv is a lake located on the border of Harju and Lääne Counties, situated in the middle of the Valgejärve hiking trail. The name "Valgejärv" (White Lake) comes from a unique lake chalk covering the lake bottom, which is three meters thick. This chalk is formed and continues to accumulate based on the lake's chalk-rich bedrock and inflowing springs. The lake has a shoreline length of 3851 meters, a surface area of 1 square kilometer, and a depth of 1 meter.

locations.title: Valgejärve Hut
locations.description: Riisipere Valgejärve vicinity features a small forest hut where visitors can take a break, relax, and enjoy the surrounding environment.

locations.title: Valgejärve Island
locations.description: In the middle of Riisipere Valgejärv, there is a small island traversed by a hiking trail, providing a beautiful view of the surrounding area.

locations.title: Pisuksenõmme Spring
locations.description: North of Valgejärv, there is Pisuksenõmme road, along which lies the Pisuksenõmme spring next to the hiking trail. Here, visitors have the opportunity to fill their water bottles if desired and continue their journey.

locations.title: Neeruti Eesjärv
locations.description: Neeruti Eesjärv is the northernmost lake in the Neeruti Twin Lakes, previously forming a single water body with Tagajärv. The combined area of this water body is 2.7 hectares, with a maximum depth of 4.9 meters. The deepest point is southeast of the lake's center, and the lake is predominantly 2-3 meters deep. The fish population mainly consists of roach, with reported presence of perch, and in small numbers, even ide and pike.

locations.title: Neeruti Tagajärv
locations.description: Neeruti Tagajärv is the southernmost lake in the Neeruti Twin Lakes, running parallel to Eesjärv but considerably longer. It covers an area of 3.2 hectares, with a maximum depth of approximately 7 meters, and the majority of the lake is around 5-6 meters deep. The fish population mainly consists of roach, with reported presence of perch, and in small numbers, even ide and pike.

locations.title: Kiissaaugumägi
locations.description: Kiisaaugumägi is a hill that signifies the farthest point of the Neeruti Hiking Trail, where the trail turns back towards the starting point.

locations.title: Sadulamägi
locations.description: Sadulamägi is located in the ridge between the Neeruti lakes. This hill fort is three-part and of the type seen in Kalevipoeg's stories, although locally it is known as Kalevipoeg's Saddle. The northwest and southeast ends of the fort are protected by ramparts approximately 2 meters high, with 5-6 meters deep and about ten meters wide ditches in front. A low embankment is also built on the sides of the fort.

locations.title: Sinijärv
locations.description: Äntu Sinijärv is the northernmost lake in the lake group, with an area of 2.4 hectares and a maximum depth of 8 meters, predominantly 6 meters in the deep area. The shores are covered with reeds, and accessing the water is challenging. Undoubtedly, Äntu Sinijärv is the bluest and clearest lake in Estonia. Even in its deepest part, the water is transparent to the bottom (8 meters). The fish population consists of perch, roach, and pike, with perch appearing to be the most numerous.

locations.title: Vahejärv
locations.description: Äntu Roheline Järv (Vahejärv) is a small lake, only 0.8 hectares in size, located between Sinijärv and Valgejärv. It has a depth of 3.3 meters and is surrounded by forest. The shores are covered with reeds, making access to the water difficult. The lakebed is covered with lake marl. The fish population includes perch, roach, and pike. Previously, the lake was abundant in crayfish.

locations.title: Valgejärv
locations.description: Äntu Valgejärv is a round-shaped lake located a few hundred meters southeast of Äntu Forest Ranger Station. It has an area of 1.4 hectares and a maximum depth of 8 meters. The shores are covered with reeds and further away with forest. There is a small swimming bridge on the western shore. The lakebed is covered with lake marl. The fish population includes perch, pike, roach, and tench.

locations.title: Linaleojärv
locations.description: Äntu Linaleojärv is a round-shaped lake located approximately 0.7 km south of Äntu Valgejärv. It covers an area of 0.25 hectares, with reed-covered shores surrounded by forest. The lake water is clear, likely sharing similar characteristics with Valgejärv. The fish species found in the lake include perch, pike, roach, and tench.

locations.title: Mäeotsa Lake
locations.description: Mäeotsa järv is a forest lake with reed-covered shores. It spans an area of 0.4 hectares, with a shoreline length of 227 meters. The lake's fish population includes perch, roach, pike, and tench.

locations.title: Äntu Punamägi
locations.description: Äntu Punamägi is a protected esker. At the southern end of the esker was the largest ancient Estonian fortress in Southern Virumaa, Agelinde, built in the early 1st millennium. It was a 240-meter-long three-part fortress consisting of front, middle, and upper fortresses. The fortresses were separated by ditches and surrounded by ramparts. Today, the protected esker serves as a reminder of the former glory of the fortress. At Äntu Punamägi, there is a camping site with a fireplace.

locations.title: Oandu-Võsu p1
locations.description: This location serves as one of the entrances to the 9.5 km Oandu-Võsu hiking trail, situated near Oandu.

locations.title: Oandu-Võsu p2
locations.description: This location serves as one of the entrances to the 9.5 km Oandu-Võsu hiking trail, situated near Võsu.

locations.title: Viitna Pikkjärv
locations.description: Viitna Pikkjärv is a lake with a surface area of 16.1 hectares and an average depth of 3 meters, and features four islands. The lake is bordered to the west by a 10-20 meter high esker. East of the lake, there are hillocks rising. The pine-covered shores are sandy or gravelly and mostly steep. There is a relatively wide sandy beach on the northern shore.The fish population is dominated by small perch, with occasional pike, some bream, tench, crucian carp and burbot.

locations.title: Nabudi Lake
locations.description: Nabudi Lake is a relatively deep, elongated lake of approximately 0.6 hectares in size, oriented in a north-south direction. It is bordered by cranberry-rich peat moss marshes on all sides, surrounded by pine forest, with alder trees found along the shore. The lake bottom is covered with a layer of silt.

locations.title: Viitna Linajärv
locations.description: Viitna Linajärv is a 4.5-hectare lake with a maximum depth of 6.7 meters. The western and especially the southern shore of Linajärv are higher, while the rest of the shore is lower, but generally firm, mostly sandy or gravelly. The fish population primarily consists of crucian carps, with both small and large individuals present. Silver bream has been introduced, but its abundance is likely not significant. There may be small numbers of perch and pike as well.

locations.title: Oandu Springs
locations.description: On the Oandu Nature Trail, you can discover Oandu Springs, which is a source of fresh water.

locations.title: Oandu Sand Dunes 1
locations.description: Here is a 7000-year-old sand dune ridge located on the Oandu Nature Trail.

locations.title: Oandu Sand Dunes 2
locations.description: Here is a 9000-year-old sand dune ridge located on the Oandu Nature Trail.

locations.title: Käsmu Kivikülv
locations.description: Next to the village of Käsmu, in the forest, is located Estonia's largest boulder field. This is a place where a large number of massive glacial erratics have accumulated. The largest boulder is Matsikivi, also known as Eremiit, which stands 4.8 meters high with a circumference of 14.7 meters. These glacial erratics were brought here by continental glaciers from Finland and Sweden around 10,000 years ago.

locations.title: Saartneem
locations.description: Saartneem is an island at the tip of the Käsmu peninsula, located about 200 meters north of the northeast end. In the summer of 2015, due to low water levels, it was possible to walk directly from the Käsmu peninsula to the island. During periods of lower water levels, it is possible to walk to the island through the water, and the island also has some forested areas.

locations.title: Palganeem
locations.description: Palganeem is the northwestern tip of the Käsmu peninsula. It offers a magnificent view of the sea, and there is an old coast guard station located at the tip of Palganeem.

locations.title: Tiirneem
locations.description: Tiirneem is a small cape situated along the Käsmu hiking trail, featuring pleasant sandy beaches in the surrounding area. Additionally, there is a shortcut from this point that allows hikers to bypass the Käsmu lake if they choose to do so.

locations.title: Käsmu Lake
locations.description: Käsmu Lake is located on the western side of the Käsmu peninsula in Lääne-Viru County, Estonia, just 200 meters away from the sea. The lake is surrounded by dry pine forest to the west and north, a mixed forest with ferns to the northeast, and a spruce-mixed forest with marshes to the southeast. The oval-shaped lake has relatively low and mostly sandy shores in the northwest-southeast direction. The shorelines are mostly muddy. The fish population in the lake includes perch, pike, bream, tench and crucian carp. Previously, ruffe and eel were also present.

locations.title: Altja Beach
locations.description: When heading north from Altja village, the road leads to the rocky Altja Beach, where you'll find many old boathouses belonging to fishermen.

locations.title: Altja Suspension Bridge
locations.description: The Altja Nature trail features a suspension bridge that spans the Altja river, offering a captivating vantage point with nice views of the surrounding beach area.

locations.title: Altja Boulder
locations.description: Altja Boulder is a large glacial erratic boulfrt on the Altja Nature Trail, standing at 4.1 meters in height and boasting a circumference of 19 meters. It was transported to this location by the glaciers during the last ice age. The boulder does not have an official name.

locations.title: Punasoo p1
locations.description: This point represents the beginning of the circular Seljamäe study trail that goes around Punasoo.

locations.title: Punasoo p2
locations.description: This marks the location of the wooden walkway situated at the heart of Punasoo, offering a broad view over the expansive bog.

locations.title: Punasoo p3
locations.description: This point is a small forested area situated in the midst of the Punasoo bog along the Seljamäe Nature Trail.

locations.title: Kõlakoja Cavern
locations.description: Kõlakoja Cavern is one of the largest caves in the Kuimetsa karst area, measuring 12 meters in length and 8 meters in width, with a cavernous chamber at the bottom of a sinkhole.

locations.title: Kuimetsa Tunnel Cave
locations.description: Kuimetsa tunnel cave is a smaller cave on the Kuimetsa hiking trail.

locations.title: Teeristi Cavern
locations.description: This is a small cavern that is located right before the crossroads. It might be a bit hard to miss at first glance.

locations.title: Kuimetsa Karst Basin
locations.description: Kuimetsa Karst Basin cave is a smaller cave on the Kuimetsa hiking trail.

locations.title: Iida Cavern
locations.description: Iida Cavern is a small cave located at the end of the Kuimetsa Hiking Trail. It might be a bit challenging to locate due to the forested surroundings.

locations.title: Loosalu Bog
locations.description: Loosalu Bog is a picturesque and classic representative of Estonian bog landscapes, featuring numerous small ponds. Easily accessible by using the wooden hiking trail.

locations.title: Loosalu Lake
locations.description: Loosalu Lake, nestled in the heart of Loosalu Bog, boasts steep and peat-covered shores that are prone to easy breakage. The lake is equipped with two small swimming piers, one on the northeast side and another on the northwest side. Perch is the dominant fish species in the lake, with fewer occurrences of pike.

locations.title: Salevere Salumägi
locations.description: Saluvere Salumägi is a coral reef compressed during the Ice Age, forming a solitary plateau as the glacier retreated and melting water sculpted the landscape. Its diverse terrain and natural surroundings make it an excellent destination to visit.

locations.title: Silmaallika Spring
locations.description: Salumägi is one of the more sacred sites in Läänemaa, primarily due to the Silmaallikas, a spring that flows from the steep slope. The water from the spring was believed to possess healing and rejuvenating properties. Visitors can have a drink from here.

locations.title: Lootsiku Rest Area
locations.description: The Penijõe hiking trail features a charming rest area near its start, nestled next to the Penijõe river. It's a perfect spot to take a break and relish the scenic surroundings.

locations.title: Saare Hill Platform
locations.description: At approximately the halfway point of the Penijõe Hiking Trail, there is a platform that offers panoramic views of the expansive fields in the vicinity.

locations.title: Kaisma Suurjärv p1
locations.description: Kaisma Lake is surrounded by marshland to the south, with some farmland in the southeast, and wet meadows and pastures to the east. The rest of the area is covered by swampy pine and mixed forests. The lake used to be significantly larger in the past but has considerably diminished and become marshy over time. The process of lake overgrowth is still ongoing. The shores are low, mostly peaty or muddy. The lake has a hiking trail that goes around it. This is the eastern point. The lake is inhabited by fish species such as roach, perch, and pike.

locations.title: Kaisma Lookout Tower
locations.description: The hiking trail around Lake Kaisma leads to an observation platform on the northern side of the lake, offering a view of Kaisma Bog. The observation platform is situated right on the edge between the bog forest and the bog itself.

locations.title: Kaisma Suurjärv p2
locations.description: Kaisma Lake is surrounded by marshland to the south, with some farmland in the southeast, and wet meadows and pastures to the east. The rest of the area is covered by swampy pine and mixed forests. The lake used to be significantly larger in the past but has considerably diminished and become marshy over time. The process of lake overgrowth is still ongoing. The shores are low, mostly peaty or muddy. The lake has a hiking trail that goes around it. This is the western point. The lake is inhabited by fish species such as roach, perch, and pike.

locations.title: Riisa Bog
locations.description: Riisa Bog is a picturesque bog located in Soomaa National Park, traversed by the Riisa hiking trail. The bog is easily accessible, and a wooden boardwalk runs through it, providing convenient passage.

locations.title: Riisa Lookout Tower
locations.description: At the far end of the Riisa Bog, you'll find the Riisa Lookout Tower, offering a magnificent view of the bog and its numerous small lakes.

locations.title: Riisa Bog Swim p1
locations.description: In Riisa Bog, numerous small bog lakes dot the landscape, and some of them offer convenient swimming access directly from the wooden walkway of the Riisa Hiking Trail.

locations.title: Riisa Bog Swim p2
locations.description: In Riisa Bog, numerous small bog lakes dot the landscape, and some of them offer convenient swimming access directly from the wooden walkway of the Riisa Hiking Trail.

locations.title: Tõramaa Lilleniit
locations.description: The Meiekose Study Trail passes through several meadows, and one of the trail's highlights is Tõramaa Flower Meadow. Here, various rare plant species can be observed during the peak flowering season in July.

locations.title: Tõramaa Tammerada
locations.description: Near the Meiekose trail, on the floodplain next to the river, grow sturdy and impressive oaks, some of which are over three hundred years old.

locations.title: Kõveri Lake
locations.description: Kõveri peat quarry lake is the start and end of the Kõveri hiking trail. The water body covers an area of 0.6 hectares, and the length of the shoreline is 343 meters.

locations.title: Timmkanal
locations.description: Timmkanal (Timm canal) is located on the Kõveri hiking trail and was excavated in the 1850s to connect the Ura River with the Rannametsa River.

locations.title: Pärapõrgu Rest area
locations.description: The Pärapõrgu Rest Area is located midway along the Kõveri hiking trail, providing a resting spot where visitors can make a campfire and enjoy the surroundings.

locations.title: Tolkuse Bog Lake
locations.description: The Tolkuse Bog Lake is one of the larger lakes in the Tolkuse Bog, and a wooden platform also provides convenient access for swimmers. The lake is located along the Tolkuse Hiking Trail.

locations.title: Nigula Lake
locations.description: Lake Nigula is at the beginning of the Nigula Bog and Nigula bog hiking trail and the surface area of the lake is 17.9 hectares, with a maximum depth of 3.1 meters. Apart from two bays reaching towards the mineral-rich ridges to the east, the lake is almost entirely surrounded by the bog. According to local residents, the lake is believed to have formed over 100 years ago during a bog fire. The lake's fish population includes a lot of small black perch, with reports of larger individuals weighing up to a kilogram. There are also mentions of pike and burbot, and some reports even suggest the presence of perch.

locations.title: Nigula Bog
locations.description: The development of the Nigula bog began approximately 10,000 years ago after the last Ice Age. This wetland formed due to the overgrowth of a large water body spanning thousands of hectares. The Nigula Bog is characterized by bog islands, which are elevated areas between the former lakes. The bog is also rich in cranberries which is also a symbol of Nigula.

locations.title: Nigula Lookout Tower
locations.description: In the heart of the Nigula Bog, there stands a lookout tower that offers a slightly elevated view of the breathtaking Nigula Bog scenery.

locations.title: Salupeaks Bog Island
locations.description: Salupeaks is a destination within the Nigula Bog. It is renowned for its well-preserved deciduous forest that has endured for about 5-6 thousand years. This forest stands as a relic from a warmer and moister climate of the past. Salupeaks is situated at the far end of a 6 km educational trail that starts from the shores of Nigula Lake, which is a relict lake in the western part of the bog.

locations.title: Rongu Bog
locations.description: Rongu Bog is a vast marsh located on the southern border of Estonia. Predominantly a flat bog with ridges, hummocks, and small ponds, it offers a relatively level terrain, allowing for extensive views from the observation platform at the end of the Sandra Hiking Trail. Positioned far from human settlements, the bog provides stunning natural vistas.

locations.title: Lilli Ash and Lungwort
locations.description: In this area of the Lilli Nature Trail, you can find tall mountain ashes reaching a height of 17 meters, as well as Lungworts on trees. The Lungworts are protected by nature conservation laws and also serve as indicators of clean air.

locations.title: Lilli Black Stork Nest
locations.description: On the Lilli Nature Trail, you can find the former nest of a black stork, which, although currently unoccupied by birds, remains a notable attraction along the trail.

locations.title: Virapuu Boulder
locations.description: Lilli Nature Trail features the Viirapuu Boulder, with a circumference of 21.8 meters.

locations.title: Teringi Bog
locations.description: Teringi Bog is a vast landscape featuring open bog areas with stunted pines, primeval forest ambiance, mixed forests, spruce groves, birch groves, pine forests, and diverse vegetation. Along the trail, starting from July, you can taste bilberries, cloudberries, blueberries, lingonberries, cranberries, and cowberries.

locations.title: Alatsi Lake
locations.description: Alatsi Lake is situated in the southeastern part of Teringi Bog. The lake is surrounded by a swampy birch forest that extends up to the water's edge, with parts of it partially dried and broken near the waterline. On the northwest shore of the lake, there is a bridge with benches, accessible via a boardwalk that leads into Teringi Bog (Teringi Nature Trail).

locations.title: Teringi Camping Tepee
locations.description: There is a tepee (a circular standing shelter) located at the southwestern corner of the Teringi hiking trail which is perched on a hillock. In the center of the standing shelter, there is a fire pit, making it an ideal spot for a hiking break to enjoy the surrounding nature.

locations.title: Õisu Põrguorg
locations.description: Õisu Hiking Trail takes you through Põrguoru, which has formed around the Vidva River flowing through the valley floor. From here, you can enjoy a scenic view of the beautiful surrounding landscape.

locations.title: Õisu Trail p1
locations.description: This point marks the furthest point of the Õisu Hiking Trail, from where the trail turns back towards the starting point.

locations.title: Öördi Lake
locations.description: On the eastern edge of Öördi Bog, there is Öördi Bog Lake with an area of 4.4 hectares. The lake used to be much larger in the past, but over time, the surrounding area became swampy, and the water level of the lake dropped to its current height. There are two platforms on the lake's shore, providing an opportunity to enjoy the view of the lake or perhaps try fishing. While the acidic water characteristic of bog lakes may not be suitable for most fish species, Öördi Lake may still have perch, pike, and roach.

locations.title: Kuresoo Lookout Tower
locations.description: The observation tower on the edge of Kuresoo Bog offers a beautiful view of the vast expanse of the bog and the forested area of Toonoja beyond the bog.

locations.title: Ingatsi Kuresoo Bog
locations.description: The Ingatsi hiking trail leads visitors directly to the enchanting mires of Kuresoo, treating them to a picturesque view of the expansive bog and its charming bog lakes. Along the way, several strategically placed rest areas provide the perfect spots to take a seat and fully immerse oneself in the serene beauty of the surroundings.

locations.title: Lemmjõe Camping Ground
locations.description: On the Lemmjõe Nature Trail, there is a simple camping area right next to the Raudna River. This cozy spot provides a fireplace with benches and a supply of firewood. It's an ideal place for a short break or spending the night surrounded by nature.

locations.title: Lemmjõe Tamm
locations.description: Lemmjõe Tamm is an oak tree at the Lemmjõe trail’s half way point next to the connection of two rivers. The Oak is over 200 years old, 23 meters high and a circumference of 5 meters.

locations.title: Kuuraniidu Forest
locations.description: The Kuuraniidu Forest is a transitional forest that has emerged from wet forest due to drainage. Characteristic features of this forest include frequent windthrow events. Spruce trunks lie uprooted on the ground, forming tall root walls. The forest is abundant with birch trees, some of which are approaching a century in age. Shade-tolerant plants like wood sorrel, wood horsetail, lily of the valley, nettle, violet, wood rush, and common wood fern thrive beneath the trees. Ferns are also prevalent. In more light-exposed clearings, blueberries and lingonberries flourish. In some areas, the forest floor is covered with a soft carpet of moss. The boardwalk is surrounded by 56 registered moss species. The Kuuraniidu Forest is home to a rich variety of mushrooms. Please be cautious during strong winds, as falling trees pose a danger.

locations.title: Tõramaa Beaver Trail p1
locations.description: The Tõramaa Beaver Trail takes a small loop through the Tõramaa forest, showcasing numerous signs of beaver activity. This point marks a half-way point of the trail.

locations.title: Hüpassaare Kuresoo Bog
locations.description: The Hüpassaare hiking trail takes you to Kuresoo Bog along a boardwalk. In this part of Kuresoo, there are numerous small bog lakes, and from the trail, you can see far across the bog, enjoying the interesting landscape. This point is roughly the half-way point of the trail.

locations.title: Parika Väikejärv
locations.description: Parika Väikejärv is renowned for its heart-shaped form, covering an area of 4.5 hectares. The Parika Väikejärv nature trail runs along the lake's shore and around it, featuring a resting area with a picnic table and a boat dock by the lake.

locations.title: Heimtali Study Trail p1
locations.description: This point marks the starting point of the Heimtali Study Trail, leading you through a forest of large linden trees, offering an intriguing natural setting along the trail.

locations.title: Heimtali Study Trail p2
locations.description: This point marks the farthest extent of the Heimtali Trail, signaling the point where the trail turns back towards the starting point.

locations.title: Soontaga Camping Ground
locations.description: On the shore of Lake Saeveski, there is the Soontaga Camping Area, equipped with multiple fireplaces, seating areas, a toilet facility, and a woodshed with an attic for sleeping. The area provides ample space, accommodating larger groups comfortably. The camping area is located at the beginning of the Soontaga Hiking Trail.

locations.title: Soontaga Trail p1
locations.description: The Soontaga Hiking Trail winds through a mixed forest and reaches the banks of the Väike-Emajõe River before turning back towards the trail's starting point.

locations.title: Saeveski Lake
locations.description: Saeveski Lake is an artificial reservoir believed to have been dammed during the Tsarist era. At one point, it was drained, but later restored. Now, it serves as a great place to spend leisure time, with a camping area along the lake and the starting point of the Soontaga Hiking Trail.

locations.title: Pühajärve Beach
locations.description: Pühajärve Beach features clean sandy shores, a swimming bridge, beach volleyball courts, playgrounds for children, and pergolas. During the summer season, the beach is patrolled by lifeguards. Pühajärve Boat Rental operates here, offering the rental of rowboats, canoes, and water bicycles. The Pühajärve Hiking Trail also passes through this area.

locations.title: Armuallikas
locations.description: The Armuallikas (love spring), also known as Veriläte (blood spring), has been recognized as a natural heritage site and is located in the Otepää Nature Park, along the Pühajärve Hiking Trail, near the Poslovitsa beach. According to folk traditions, the spring got its name from the magical properties of its water: drinking from a silver cup on Midsummer Night is believed to create lasting and deep love. Additionally, the water from this spring is said to alleviate pain and promote healing for the sick.

locations.title: Pühajärve Sõjatamm
locations.description: Pühajärve Sõjatamm (Pühajärve War Oak) got its name from the 1841 peasant uprising against the landlord of Pühajärve Manor. According to information from the information board near the oak, its circumference is 698 cm, height is 22 meters, and it is estimated to be 300-400 years old. The oak is under nature protection.

locations.title: Murrumets
locations.description: Murrumets is a forest located near Pühajärve, traversed by the Murrumetsa hiking trail, aimed at introducing the flora and fauna of our forests and cultural landscapes.

locations.title: Taagepera Ohvrikivi
locations.description: The Taagepera Ohvrikivi (Victims' Stone) is a glacial erratic located along the Taagepera hiking trail, with a circumference of 11.8 meters. The stone acquired its name from the historical use of its flat top for sacrificial purposes. It might be a bit difficult to locate.

locations.title: Taagepera Cemetery
locations.description: Here, you'll find the Taagepera Cemetery, which is the furthest point of the Taagepera hiking trail, from which the trail begins to loop back.

locations.title: Jalase Lake
locations.description: At the end of the Jalase hiking trail in the Sõbesoo bog, you'll find Jalase Lake, covering an area of 2.5 hectares. Sõbessoo, with its many hollows, serves as a suitable nesting place for typical bog birds. Here, you might observe skylarks or marsh harriers. The surrounding forests are home to various woodpecker species, hazel grouses, capercaillies, woodcocks, and owls. In the summer, the blooming water lilies on the lake's surface provide a delightful sight. On a hot summer day, it's a wonderful opportunity for a refreshing swim.

locations.title: Mukri Bog
locations.description: The Mukri Bog near Eidapere was formed approximately 10,000 to 9,000 years ago when a lake became overgrown with peat, making it one of the oldest bogs in Estonia. In the bog, you'll find Mukri Lake, a 2.2-hectare water body adorned with islets and water lilies. Those unafraid of the dark water can take a swim here.

locations.title: Vilbaste Springs
locations.description: The deep source basin on the right bank of the Võllinge stream's eastern shore creates the Vilbaste Springs. At the bottom of the basin, blue sand and fallen branches are visible. The basin is surrounded by peat mud at the bottom of the recess. The area around the spring is covered with a boggy coniferous forest. The Võllinge Spring hiking trail leads to the spring from the east, running along a boardwalk along the northern side of the spring.

locations.title: Seli Lake
locations.description: Seli Lake is a shallow, overgrowing lake with wide reed beds situated in the middle of Seli Bog. The water surface covers 17.6 hectares, the shoreline is 1780 meters long, the average depth is 1 meter, and the maximum depth is 1.5 meters.

locations.title: Kakerdaja Bog
locations.description: In Kakerdaja Bog, numerous small ponds, known as “laukad”, can be found. In the central part of the bog lies Kakerdi Lake, covering an area of 6.7 hectares. During spring and autumn, thousands of waterfowl make a stopover in the bog's pond-studded landscape. With its brownish-tinted ponds and the pine bogs growing along their shores, the bog offers a picturesque scene. The bog is named after the Eurasian curlew, locally known as the "kakerdaja.

locations.title: Kakerdi Lake
locations.description: In the center of Kakerdaja Bog lies a highly acidic, rainwater-fed peat lake called Kakerdi Bog Lake. The lake covers an area of 6.7 hectares. The water in the lake is highly acidic, deriving nutrients from precipitation and bog water. The lake's water is brownish-red and has low transparency (1.6 meters). The lake is devoid of any vegetation.

locations.title: Kakerdaja Bog Platform
locations.description: In Kakerdaja Bog, numerous small ponds, known as “laukad”, can be found. In the central part of the bog lies Kakerdi Lake, covering an area of 6.7 hectares. During spring and autumn, thousands of waterfowl make a stopover in the bog's pond-studded landscape. With its brownish-tinted ponds and the pine bogs growing along their shores, the bog offers a picturesque scene. The bog is named after the Eurasian curlew, locally known as the "kakerdaja. Here is a wooden platform to take a break and enjoy the serene surroundings of the bog.

locations.title: Väätsa Bog
locations.description: While walking along the Väätsa hiking trail, you'll encounter a section of the ancient winter road connecting Paide and Tallinn. This 1.2 km stretch through the bog reveals well-preserved remnants of the winter road. During the manor era, ditches were created to connect the ponds in the center of the bog, and these ditches are still visible today. The purpose was to irrigate the hay meadows upstream along the Lokuta River. The area between the altered water regime of the ponds is covered by a sparsely occurring 100-year-old bog pine forest.

locations.title: Uljaste Oos
locations.description: Above Uljaste Lake, there is a 20 m high esker, which is arc-shaped with a narrow and sharp crest. South of the lake, the esker widens, and its surface is undulating. The absolute height of the esker's southern part is 89.7 m. Uljaste esker was formed in glacial crevices around 12,000 years ago during the late Ice Age. It is also the highest natural peak in Ida-Virumaa.

locations.title: Uljaste Järv
locations.description: The shores of Uljaste Lake are steep, sandy, and gravelly near the esker, while near the bog, they are covered with peat. The lake covers an area of approximately 60 hectares, with a depth of 5-6 meters. It is a brownish-yellow to greenish-yellow eutrophic lake, characterized by relatively low water transparency (1.5-1.8 meters). The lake is home to a diverse fish population, including perch, roach, ide, tench, pike, bream, and others. Uljaste Lake is situated alongside the Uljaste hiking trail.

locations.title: Saka Cambrian Terrace
locations.description: Saka Cambrian Terrace is a point on the Saka hiking trail where a staircase leads from the top of Saka Cliff down to the beach. Here, you can observe the surrounding nature and the changing landscape.

locations.title: Valaste Trail p1
locations.description: One end of the Valaste Waterfall hiking trail starts here. From this point, a staircase descends along the cliff from the nearby parking lot towards Valaste Waterfall.

locations.title: Toila-Martsa Trail p1
locations.description: This point marks the Martsa end of the longer trail of the Toila-Martsa hiking trail.

locations.title: Toila Campfire Ground
locations.description: Toila campfire site is located near Toila, in a forest next to the beach. It features a covered fireplace, roofed tables and benches, lean-tos, a dry toilet, and a woodshed. The Toila-Marta hiking trail also passes nearby.

locations.title: Toila Beach
locations.description: Toila Beach is located in the Ida-Viru Central Region, on the coast of the Gulf of Finland. The beach is accessible via a winding pedestrian path through Oru Park. The beach is characterized by flat stone embankments alongside a narrow strip of sand, forming a gravel beach geologically. There are 2 beach volleyball courts, a performance court, and beach swings at the beach.

locations.title: Narva jõe Camping Ground
locations.description: The Narva River campfire site is situated at the picturesque intersection of the Poruni and Narva rivers, on the edge of the Puhatu bog. It features a covered fireplace with a grill grate, four benches, lean-tos, a woodshed, a roofed picnic table, and a dry toilet. This area is home to many rare animal and plant species, including eagles and capercaillies. Note: The only access is from the Jõhvi–Vasknarva highway.

locations.title: Martiska Camping Ground
locations.description: The campfire site is located on the shore of Lake Martiska, whose water level has decreased the most among the Kurtna Lakes over the decades due to industrial influence. The lake has an area of 2.5 hectares and a maximum depth of 9 meters. There is an information board, a dry toilet, 2 covered campfire sites, and 2 roofed picnic tables.,

locations.title: Ahnejärv
locations.description: Kurtna Ahnejärv is a hollow surrounded by sandy hills. The shores are firm, mostly sandy, covered mainly with pine forest and occasionally with marshy areas. On the western shore, there is the rare porcupine grass found in Eastern Estonia. The bottom of Ahnejärv has an uneven bottom, covered with sand in the shoreline zone, occasionally with peat, and deeper areas have very black gyttja. There are many reeds and pondweed in the water. It is primarily a dystrophic lake, nourished by precipitation. The water is light green, exceptionally transparent (6.0-6.1 m), making it more transparent than any other lake in Kurtna. Thanks to its good transparency, the water should not be sharply stratified. The lake is inhabited by perch.

locations.title: Särgjärv
locations.description: Kurtna Särgjärv is a 2.4-hectare elongated lake located on the eastern edge of the Kurtna mire complex. It is situated in a marshy area with deciduous and mixed forests and has low peaty shores. The lake bottom appears to be very muddy. The throughflow in the water body is relatively strong, with water coming in from ditches connected to Lake Nõmmejärv and Lake Punane, and outflow occurring towards Lake Ahvenjärv. The lake is not particularly rich in vegetation, with only 17 species identified. There is a layer of reeds along the shoreline, indicating the water's richness in iron. Fish species present in the lake include perch, roach, pike, crucian carp, and bream.

locations.title: Nõmmejärv
locations.description: Kurtna Nõmmejärv is a 12.6-hectare lake with an average depth of 3.1 meters and a maximum depth of 7.5 meters. The lake has clear water and abundant nutrients. Located on the western edge of the Kurtna mire complex, the lake is surrounded by sandy hills covered in coniferous forests to the east, south, and southwest, with some agricultural land to the southwest. The eastern shore, providing swimming opportunities, is mainly sandy, while the rest of the shoreline is peat-muddy, covered with brownish sapropel at greater depths. Nõmmejärve hosts a rich variety of aquatic plants, comprising 18 different species. The lake is home to a diverse fish population, including roach, bream, pike, perch, ruffe, tench, burbot, and minnow. While the lake used to be inhabited by crayfish, a decrease in water levels has left the crayfish burrows dry.

locations.title: Mustjärv
locations.description: Kurtna Mustjärv is a warm lake surrounded by bog and bog pine on its shores. After the construction of the ditch connecting it to Lake Nõmmejärv, the water level in this lake has decreased by more than 0.5 meters. However, the lake's surface area has not significantly reduced despite the water level drop, remaining at 5.6 hectares.

locations.title: Niinsaare Lake
locations.description: Niinsaare Lake, located 0.5 km northwest of Lake Nõmmejärv, was a warm lake until it was significantly impacted by ditch excavation in 1963. The lake, once 7.7 hectares in size, is now surrounded mainly by transition bogs, with only the eastern part bordered by a bog. The water level dropped significantly after the excavation of ditches connecting it to Kurtna Suurjärv and Mustjärv. In the summer of 1968, at least 50% of the lake had dried up.

locations.title: Peenjärv
locations.description: Kurtna Suurjärv is divided into the northern part called Suurjärv and the southern part called Väikejärv or Peenjärv. The deepest point is in the central part of Suurjärv, but Väikejärv also has a depth of 6.5 meters in the middle. The lake is surrounded by transitioning boggy forests, bogs, or fen, with high forest-covered sandy ridges extending to the southeast. The sandy shore belt is present in this area. Elsewhere, the shores are low, muddy, or peaty. The bottom is mostly covered with peat mud. The lake primarily receives water from precipitation and the surrounding swamp, and there are likely underwater springs. The water is yellowish-brown or brownish-yellow with moderate transparency (1.9-2.5 meters), mixing well and warming easily. Suurjärv is relatively rich in fish, hosting perch, bream, roach, pike, bleak, and tench. It was once known as a very good crayfish lake, but not much of the crayfish population has been preserved.

locations.title: Selisoo Bog
locations.description: Selisoo is a raised bog characterized by bog pines, hummocks, and well-defined bog pools. The Selisoo hiking trail, located in Ida-Viru County, is one of the region's most interesting bog trails, offering easy access. The trail introduces visitors to various types of bogs, including transition bogs, blanket bogs, and forested bogs.

locations.title: Selisoo Camping Shelter
locations.description: At Selisoo, near Lake Seli, there is a lean-to shelter, providing open and unfurnished accommodation where visitors can seek shelter from rain or even stay overnight.

locations.title: Luite Trail p1
locations.description: The Luite hiking trail eventually reaches the mouth of the Rannapungerja River and then turns back towards the beginning of the trail.

locations.title: Kauksi Oja p1
locations.description: The Kauksi Stream hiking trail follows along one bank of Kauksi Stream and eventually, the trail crosses the stream using a bridge, leading back along the opposite bank. This point marks the bridge where the trail turns away from the shore.

locations.title: Kauksi Oja p2
locations.description: The Kauksi Stream hiking trail follows along one bank of Kauksi Stream and eventually, the trail crosses the stream using a bridge, leading back along the opposite bank. This point marks the bridge where the trail turns back towards the shore.

locations.title: Seljandiku Camping Hut
locations.description: The Seljandiku Forest Hut is located on a sand ridge formed during the Ice Age, offering beautiful views. The landscape is mosaic-like due to the sand ridges, and the transitions between ecosystems are abrupt. This area has been a site of historical battles, including conflicts in February-March 1944. The camping area here includes the forest hut, a shelter with tables and benches, a woodshed, a dry toilet, a covered fireplace with a grill, and a garbage bin.

locations.title: Rüütli Bog
locations.description: The Kotka Hiking Trail passes through Rüütli Bog, where you can observe intriguing bog islands formed from deposits accumulated at the end of the Ice Age. The trail also showcases changes in the landscape as it progresses from Rüütli Bog to sandy ridges.

locations.title: Kotka Lookout Tower
locations.description: Kotka Observation Tower is a structure with a approximately 10 m high viewing platform. The tower features a single-pitched wooden structure with a hot-dip galvanized corrugated metal roof, designed to direct sunlight to the center of the structure and is noticeable from a distance due to its gleaming tip. It provides an excellent view of the surrounding Rüütli Bog.

locations.title: Männikjärv
locations.description: Männikjärv is located on the eastern edge of the marsh, bordered by marshland to the west and lowland to the south and east. The lake has an oval shape with a very shallow depth of up to 3 m, with at least 1.1 m being soft mud. The water is yellow to brownish-yellow, with visibility ranging from clear to muddy (1.3 m). The lake bottom is almost entirely covered with vegetation, especially common are shoreline and submerged plants. Männikjärv is the only place in Estonia where the very rare spiny water nymph (Najas intermedia) grows. The lake is home to perch, roach, and tench, with pike and bream being less common.

locations.title: Endla Bog Crossroad
locations.description: The Endla Bog Crossroad is the intersection where the Männikjärve Bog Hiking Trail and the Endla Lake Hiking Trail meet.

locations.title: Endla Lake
locations.description: Endla Lake is bordered to the north by Linnusaare Bog, to the west by Endla Bog, to the east by a birch-covered bog, and elsewhere by low and transitional marshes. Only the northeast side has a firmer shore, where a bush-covered peninsula extends, featuring the well-known Juta Stone (diameter 2 m) from mythology. To the east of this peninsula lies a mineral mound covered with beautiful alder groves. The position and size of Endla Lake have constantly changed. Since the Subatlantic period, the lake has expanded somewhat to the south and east due to the encroachment of bogs, evidenced by the stumps preserved along the shore. The water level of the lake has been lowered twice. There are a couple of springs near Juta Stone. The brownish-yellow water is not very transparent (0.7-0.9 m). The lake is home to perch, roach, pike, and tench, as well as whitefish, bream, vendace, burbot, and smelt.

locations.title: Männikjärve Bog
locations.description: Männikjärve Bog is a part of the Endla Nature Reserve, featuring numerous crescent-shaped bog pools, totaling over 120 in Männikjärve Bog. The bog is traversed by the Männikjärve Bog Hiking Trail.

locations.title: Pedja Camping Ground
locations.description: The Pedja River campsite at the beginning of the Kirna Study Trail is a well-known resting spot for river enthusiasts and a pleasant resting place for trail visitors. The camping area features a covered fireplace with a grill grate, a shelter, four benches, and a two-person outdoor toilet.

locations.title: Kirna Suspension Bridge
locations.description: At a more distant point on the Kirna Hiking Trail, visitors can cross the river using the suspension bridge located here.

locations.title: Pedassaar
locations.description: The Tammeluha Hiking Trail leads to Pedassaar, located in the middle of three lakes. Pedassaar is covered by a diverse mixed forest with over 100 years of age and rich ground vegetation. It stands slightly higher than the surrounding area, allowing for forest growth, while the land before Pedassaar is used as a meadow. The site offers a magnificent view of the surrounding lakes and provides an opportunity to explore the local nature.

locations.title: Alatskivi Lake
locations.description: The reservoir in Alatskivi, located in the town, was initially created for a mill on the Alatskivi River during the Swedish era, in the 19th century. The local landowner expanded the lake's surface, resulting in a two-part lake with a total area of 15 hectares by the early 20th century (Upper, low-lying Veskijärv, 8 hectares, Lower, oval-shaped, high-banked Lossijärv, 7 hectares). In 1973, the lake was approximately 3 meters deep near the dam. The water is olive-yellow, with average transparency (1.5 meters). The lake is said to be rich in fish and serves for swimming and fishing activities, with ducks overwintering here. The Alatskivi hiking trail passes by the lake.

locations.title: Alatskivi Lake Bridge
locations.description: Alatskivi Lake is divided into two parts, and a bridge between them offers a beautiful view of both lakes.

locations.title: Alatskivi Kalevipoja Säng
locations.description: Kalevipoja Säng is a hillfort located on a steep ridge, standing 10-15 meters high. It once housed a fortress and is situated at the northeastern foothill of Alatskivi River. The earlier settlement phase dates back to the Early Metal Age, with its main period of use in the late first millennium and the beginning of the second millennium.

locations.title: Suuretüki Lookout Tower
locations.description: Suuretüki Lookout Tower is a 12-meter-tall wooden observation tower located on the edge of Laeva Swamp, on the land of the former Suuretüki farm. The tower provides picturesque views of the swamp and the boardwalk that runs through it, offering different atmospheres throughout the seasons.

locations.title: Laeva Bog
locations.description: Selli-Sillaotsa Hiking Trail leads to Laeva Bog, showcasing various stages of bog development and known for its abundant cranberry spots during the right season.

locations.title: Tamme Paljand
locations.description: Tamme Paljand is a sandstone outcrop on the eastern shore of Lake Võrtsjärv. The sandstone at the outcrop features large and small holes. Swallows nest in the larger cavities, while smaller holes are excavated by mining bees.

locations.title: Tamme Paljand Beach
locations.description: At the end of the Tamme Paljand hiking trail, there is a beach where visitors can also swim.

locations.title: Valguta Mustjärv
locations.description: Valguta Mustjärve is a shallow lake with a maximum depth of 1 meter. The lake is surrounded by peat bogs, extensive fens to the south, and bog pine forests in other directions. The water surface is mostly surrounded by a bog margin of peat moss, with easy access to the water at the turbid shore to the north. Beyond the lake, there are pine and mixed forests. The lakebed is covered with a thick layer of mud (up to 3 meters), beneath which lies a layer of sand. The water of the lake has a brown color, and its transparency is limited to about 0.7 meters. The dominant fish species in the lake is the large and beautiful tench, with pike also present. The Mustjärve hiking trail leads to the lake.

locations.title: Emajõe Trail p1
locations.description: Emajõe Hiking Trail takes a loop around the riverside meadow, the marshy lowland meadow with cotton-grass, and passes through a swampy grove. At this point, the trail goes through a swampy grove.

locations.title: Emajõe Trail p2
locations.description: Emajõe Hiking Trail takes a loop around the riverside meadow, the marshy lowland meadow with cotton-grass, and passes through a swampy grove. At this point, the trail passes by the riverside meadow and turns back into the forest.

locations.title: Vapramägi
locations.description: Vapramägi is a steep hill in Tartu County, Estonia. The absolute height of the hill is 79.7 meters, with a relative height of about 40 meters compared to the water level of the Elva River flowing at the foot of the hill. There are several legends and folk traditions associated with the hill, including stories that claim Kalevipoeg, a legendary hero, defeated the monster Tõll on Vapramägi. On Vapramägi, there is a protected concrete memorial stone dedicated to two Chinese soldiers who were part of the Red Army's mine battalion during the Estonian War of Independence. They fell in the Battle of Vapramägi on January 16, 1919, attacking the Estonian army armored train near the Voika railway bridge. The memorial stone bears inscriptions in Estonian, Russian, and Chinese: "Eternal glory to the brave Chinese Red Army soldiers who fell in the fight for Soviet power!”

locations.title: Peedu Hillfort
locations.description: Peedu Hillfort is located on the highest and steepest part of the hill. A ditch at both ends separates the fortress area from the rest of the hill. Peedu Hillfort is one of the smaller fortresses in Estonia: it is 48 meters long and up to 17 meters wide, with an inner courtyard area of only 650 square meters. The height of the steep 25-degree slopes is 11–12 meters in the northwest and 15–16 meters in the southeast. The embankments that were once on the edges of the fortress have been destroyed over time, likely due to leveling efforts. The fortress dates back to the 6th–11th centuries and was in use during that time period.

locations.title: Vapramäe Camping Ground
locations.description: The camping area on the Vapramäe hiking trail is situated directly on the banks of the Elva River, providing a peaceful spot to rest, make a campfire, and enjoy the surrounding area. The camping area features a designated fire pit, multiple seating areas, and a woodshed for convenience.

locations.title: Vapramäe Lookout Point
locations.description: The Vapramäe hiking trail introduces Vapramägi and its surroundings, featuring a viewpoint at this location that offers a panoramic view over the Elva River flowing beside Vapramägi. This allows visitors to better appreciate the natural beauty of the area.

locations.title: Umbjärve Camping Ground
locations.description: Umbjärve campsite, nestled in a beautiful pine forest south of Elva, along the lake, serves as a picturesque spot for short breaks, swims, or extended stays. The site offers amenities such as a shelter with benches, covered fireplace with a grill, additional benches, a wood shelter, a restroom, and a pier by the lake. The surrounding forest features numerous trails, providing opportunities for delightful walks.

locations.title: Ürgoru Camping Ground
locations.description: The campsite is situated atop the Elva Primeval Valley sandstone outcrop, locally known as Liivamägi. This location resembles the local "Taevaskoda" (Heaven's Hall): below, the Elva River flows through the deep gorge, and from the top, there's a picturesque view of the surroundings. The campsite serves as a stopping point for those on the long Elva River Primeval Valley trail or the shorter Viti Lake hiking trail, or for anyone simply enjoying a forest retreat, offering amenities such as two sheltered tables with benches, a covered fireplace with a grill, additional benches, stairs, cliff barriers, a wood shelter, and a restroom.

locations.title: Valgesoo Bog
locations.description: Valgesoo is primarily a raised bog, with only two open areas where heath-like vegetation grows. The bog has a flat surface covered with bog pine. The transition from the bog to mineral soil is quite abrupt, and only a narrow strip of transitional bog forest borders the edge of the bog. The average depth of the peat layer is 1.3 meters. The peat layer lies directly on sand, indicating that the bog formed as a result of the peatification of a sandy depression.

locations.title: Saesaare Hydropower Plant
locations.description: The Saesaare Hydropower plant is situated along the Ahja River, constructed in 1949. It serves as the starting point for the Taevaskodade hiking trail, while the Kiidjärve hiking trail leads back to its origin from here.

locations.title: Roiupalu Palanumägi
locations.description: Palanumägi is located in the landscape protection area of the Ahja River's primeval valley, on the eastern side of the river's loop. Palanumägi is a sandstone outcrop, approximately 100 meters long, situated along the Roiupalu hiking trail. The outcrop has a steep wall, dropping directly into the water, and reaches a height of up to 8 meters.

locations.title: Sõnajala Mägi
locations.description: Sõnajala Mägi (mägi means hill) is a 12-meter-high and 40-meter-long sandstone outcrop that drops steeply into the water. Situated in the landscape protection area of the Ahja River's primeval valley, Sõnajala Mägi is crossed by the Roiupalu hiking trail.

locations.title: Tilleorg
locations.description: Tilleorg is a valley located on the upper course of the Ahja River in Põlva County. The valley is 400–500 meters wide and up to 35 meters deep, featuring slopes with angles ranging from 10 to 26 degrees. The right bank of the valley is unjointed and steeper, while the left bank has terraces and is gentler. Several additional valleys also merge into the main valley from the left side. The name "Tilleorg" comes from the fact that the upper course of the Ahja River is referred to as Tille River or Tille Creek. The Tilleorg hiking trail passes through the Tilleorg valley.

locations.title: Merioone Spring
locations.description: Merioone Spring is located near the mouth of Varbuse River in the cliff outcrop on the western slope of an ancient valley. The spring also marks the furthest point of the trail. The spring is quite abundant, with a flow rate of up to 20 liters per second.

locations.title: Meenikunno Bog
locations.description: Meenikunno Bog, located in Põlvamaa, has an area of 1448 hectares and a maximum peat thickness of 6 meters (average 2.6 meters). The southwestern part of the bog formed after the Ice Age as a result of a water body overgrowing, while the rest of the bog developed on sand-covered peat deposits. There are also some warm lakes in the bog. The Meenikunno Hiking Trail provides an introduction to this bog.

locations.title: Suur Suurjärv
locations.description: Suur Suujärv is a dystrophic lake located in Meenikunnu Bog. It is a relatively circular-shaped, warm lake with an area of 4.7 hectares and a depth ranging from 2 to 3 meters. There is also an observation platform by the lake where visitors can sit and enjoy the surroundings.

locations.title: Kamarusjärv
locations.description: Kamarusjärv is a bog lake located in the Meenikunnu Bog, situated near Kamarusmägi. At the top of Kamarusmägi, there is a fee-based forest house offering accommodation called Päikeseloojangu Metsamaja. From the forest house, a wooden staircase leads to Kamarusjärv, providing a beautiful view, especially during sunset.

locations.title: Piusa Clearing
locations.description: Piusa Caves is the beginning of a hiking trail that leads through the Piusa nature. At this point the trail passes by a clearing, offering views of the surrounding lowland from on top of sand hills.

locations.title: Piusa Trail p1
locations.description: This marks the point where the Piusa hiking trail turns away from the clearing toward the forested area.

locations.title: Härma Camping Ground
locations.description: The "Härma Alumine" or "Kõlgusniidu" cliff is a 20.5 m high protruding rock in the Piusa Primeval Valley, celebrated for its vibrant-colored clay layers and majestic pyramidal shape. In the meadow by the river bend, there's a cozy fireplace area where camping and making a fire are possible. The amenities include an open fireplace with a grill grate, table-benches with a canopy, a shelter for wood, and a restroom.

locations.title: Kirikumäe Lake
locations.description: The lake got its name from the Orthodox church that was located on the northern shore during the Livonian War. It has an area of 62 hectares and a maximum depth of 3.5 meters (average depth of 2.8 meters). The immediate surroundings of the lake are low and swampy, with larger and smaller hills and ridges rising to the north. There is a swimming opportunity on the northern shore. The Kirikumäe hiking trail surrounds the lake, introducing the local nature and the lake itself. The dominant fish species in the lake are perch and roach, with pike, vendace, ide, and tench also present.

locations.title: Kisejärv
locations.description: Kisejärv is characterized by a very indented shoreline. A sprawling peninsula, Vaibsaar, extends into the lake from the southwest, dividing the lake into three parts. The immediate vicinity of Kisejärv is flat, but further away, there are mounds and eskers formed by meltwater from the ice. The lake is mostly surrounded by a forested cultural landscape, with a boggy pine forest reaching the lake in the south and southeast. The shores are shallow, peaty, and in the northern and northeastern parts, sandy. The lake has a variety of fish species, with perch and roach being the most abundant. Other species present include pike, bream, ide, tench, whitefish, and burbot. Once known for its crayfish population, crayfish are no longer present in significant numbers. Archeological discoveries have been made in Kisejärv, including boats and fishing gear dating back up to a thousand years.

locations.title: Luikjärv
locations.description: Located barely 100 meters southwest of Kisejärv, Luikjärv is a forest lake with boggy shores. It covers an area of 3.5 hectares, with a depth of 4 meters. The lake bottom is covered with mud, and it is characterized as a peat lake. The water in Luikjärv is brownish-yellow, with moderate transparency (1.8 meters), and likely has minimal stratification. The lake bottom is covered with mud.

locations.title: Pahijärv
locations.description: Despite aging and becoming nutrient-poor, Pahijärv, with a depth of only 2 meters, sulfuric content in its bottom, and warming due to peat, has preserved over 15 species of rare algae typically found only in clean, cold-water lakes.

locations.title: Vällamägi
locations.description: Vällamägi, the second-highest hill in Estonia, is located on the Haanja upland. Its elevation is 301.6 meters above sea level, and it boasts the highest relative height among Estonia's natural hills, with a height from base to summit of 84 meters. Geomorphologically, Vällamägi is a typical drumlin in Haanja, composed of moraine-covered glacial meltwater sediments. The slopes are extremely steep, reaching up to 40 degrees, and show signs of erosion with some forming eskers. The slopes of Vällamägi are classified as moderately to strongly erosion-prone. The hill is covered with ancient natural forest. At the foot of the hill lies a bog with a 17-meter-thick peat layer and several small transitional bogs, believed to have been lakes in the past.

locations.title: Ööbikuorg
locations.description: Ööbikuorg is a valley in southern Estonia with a length of 300 meters and a depth of 12–15 meters. It is traversed by a small hiking trail known as Ööbikuoru matkarada. During the spring, especially during the blooming of bird cherries, the valley echoes with the song of nightingales, which is the source of the valley's name, translating to "Nightingale Valley."

locations.title: Rõuge Suurjärv
locations.description: Rõuge Suurjärv, also known as Nõiajärv, is situated in a deep primeval valley, belonging to the Rõuge chain of lakes. It holds the distinction of being the deepest lake in Estonia, with depths reaching up to 38 meters. Along the shores of Suurjärv, there is a well-maintained public sandy beach, and the Rõuge primeval valley hiking trail runs along the lakeshore. The lake's fish population primarily consists of perch and roach, with other species such as pike, whitefish, minnow, ide, and dace also present. Rainbow trout, vendace, and Peipsi whitefish have been introduced into the lake.

locations.title: Ratasjärv
locations.description: Rõuge Ratasjärv, located in the town of Rõuge, was historically known as Rattaaluse järv (named after a waterwheel that was once operated at its inlet). The lake has a surface area of 6.3 hectares, with a maximum depth of 19 meters and an average depth of 8.5 meters. Parts of the lake are surrounded by a marshy strip, and there is a sandy shore in the central part of the northeastern shore. The lake's bottom is covered by a mud layer up to 4.6 meters thick. The current fish population in Ratasjärv is reported to be limited. In the past, the lake was known to have perch, roach, pike, whitefish, bream, and eel, among other species.

locations.title: Tõugjärv
locations.description: Tõugjärv is a heart-shaped lake with a surface area of 4.2 hectares and a maximum depth of 17 meters (average depth 7.6 meters). The shores are elevated, covered with forest to the east, and fields to the west, while the immediate shoreline is muddy or marshy. Similar to Ratasjärv, Tõugjärv has become relatively fish-poor. In the past, the lake was known to have perch, roach, pike, whitefish, bream, eel, among other species.

locations.title: Kahrila Lake
locations.description: Kahrila Lake is a valley lake with a length slightly over 2.3 km and a maximum width of only 270 m. The slopes of the ancient valley are high, especially above the southeast end of the lake, where they reach heights of up to 73 m above sea level. Several additional valleys, with Enni Valley being the most significant, cut into the slopes. The slopes are predominantly occupied by farms and farmland, with some areas covered by bushes and forests. There are abundant clay and sandy shores, and the southeast and north ends of the lake have marshy shores. Kahrila is one of the best fishing lakes in Rõuge, with species such as perch, pike, roach, ruffe, whitefish, vimba bream, ide, bream, tench, eel, carp, whitefish, and vendace.

locations.title: Luhasoo Bog
locations.description: Luhasoo is the only bog protection area in Võrumaa, and it is interesting because it allows you to observe all the stages of bog formation, from wet lowland areas to high bogs. To explore Luhasoo, there is a 5-kilometer-long educational trail, and walking in its wet lowland and bog sections is facilitated by a boardwalk.

locations.title: Luhasuu Mustjärv
locations.description: In the middle of the bog, there is Mustjärv (Black Lake). As remnants of a once larger lake, there are now three bog lakes remaining. The hiking trail reaches the shores of Mustjärv, and there are several lakes with the name Mustjärv (Black Lake) in Estonia. Folklore tells eerie tales about such dark places. For instance, it is said that in the Mustjärv located in Luhasoo, there is an old devil residing there. People claim to have seen him running around the lake, especially during thunderstorms when lightning bolts are said to chase him. Additionally, there are warnings not to drink the water of Mustjärv, as it is believed to bring death near the mouth.

locations.title: Luhasoo Camping Hut
locations.description: Luhasoo Bog in Estonia features a mineral-rich island with a cozy forest cabin. The cabin is equipped with platforms and a fireplace. Adjacent to the forest cabin, there is a separate designated fireplace. To make a fire, visitors are advised to bring firewood from the shelter located at the beginning of the hiking trail on the boardwalk.

locations.title: Raadi Lookout Tower
locations.description: The 24-meter-high wooden observation tower on Raadi Hill provides a panoramic view of the enchanting landscape of Paganamaa and the plains of Latvia beyond the chain of lakes.

locations.title: Liivajärv
locations.description: The third link in the chain of lakes, Paganamaa Liivajärv, covers an area of 4.2 hectares, of which 2.0 hectares belong to Estonia. The maximum depth of the lake is 19.8 meters. Located in the midst of the forest, the northern slope of the valley is particularly high and steep above the lake. The shores are firm, occasionally sandy, and the water deepens abruptly. Liivajärv has the clearest water among the lakes in Paganamaa, with a transparency of 3.7 meters and a greenish-yellow color. The fish population is similar to other lakes in Paganamaa, including pike, perch, bream, and whitefish.

locations.title: Sarapuujärv
locations.description: Sarapuu järv is one of the four lakes in Paganamaa, with a depth of up to 8.8 meters and a size of 2.5 hectares. Fish species found in the lake include whitefish, pike, bream, perch, roach, tench, and burbot.

locations.title: Kikkajärv
locations.description: Kikkajärv is the northeasternmost lake in the chain of lakes, with a length of 1 km. Of its total area of 20.4 hectares, 12 hectares belong to the Republic of Estonia. A picturesque island with birch trees in the eastern part of the lake belongs to Latvia. The lake's deepest point (22.5 m) is northwest of the island. The slopes of the valley rise very high above the lake at an angle of 15-20 degrees. On the Estonian side, there is the 166 m high Kikkamägi hill, and the Latvian side of the valley appears to be even higher. The shores are mostly firm, and the water deepens sharply. Fish species in the lake include pike, perch, bream, and whitefish.

locations.title: Piiriorg
locations.description: Piiriorg is located in the Paganamaa Landscape Conservation Area, with the Peeli River flowing through its center. The surrounding nature and the valley itself are explored by the Paganamaa hiking trail. The valley runs in an east-west direction, with a depth of 59 m and a width of 200-1200 m. Four deep lakes are situated in the Piiriorg valley: Kikkajärv, Sarapuujärv, Liivajärv, and Mudajärv.

locations.title: Ähijärv
locations.description: Ähijärv is one of the significant lakes in Karula, measuring 176.2 hectares. While it is relatively large, with a maximum depth of 5.5 meters and an average depth of 3.8 meters, it is considered shallow. The shores feature beautiful elevations, including Suurmägi to the east, Tammemägi to the west, and forested sandy areas to the south. Predominantly, there are farmlands in the northeast and east, while the rest is mostly covered by forests and meadows. The shores vary from gentle to some steep areas, with shallow bays and inlets having sandy-gravel and muddy bottoms. Ähijärv is known as an excellent fishing lake with abundant species such as bream, perch, pike, ide, roach, whitefish, burbot, and eel.

locations.title: Kütioru Trail p1
locations.description: This point serves as a signpost for marking the Kütioru hiking trail. At this location, the trail crosses the Iskna River, around which Kütiorg has formed.

locations.title: Kütioru Trail p2
locations.description: This point serves as a signpost for marking the Kütioru hiking trail. At this location, the trail passes by Ilmamägi, where the former sacrificial site of Tammõtsõõr is located.

locations.title: Alaveski Lake
locations.description: This point serves as a signpost for marking the Kütioru hiking trail. At this location, the trail passes by Alaveski Lake, which is a public reservoir with a shoreline length of 447 meters.

locations.title: Kütioru Trail p3
locations.description: This point serves as a signpost for marking the Kütioru hiking trail. At this location, the trail turns into or out of the forest, depending on the direction from which one is coming.

locations.title: Kõrgema Võluallikas
locations.description: This is the location of a secluded and ancient forest, a sacred grove of the ancient Sõrve people where the Kõrgema Spring is located.
