Anyone can have a sip of the spring water or take a bit with them. The spring flows from within the “Kõrgema” (Higher) Sand Ridge, hence its name. Unfortunately, the spring has often run dry during the summer in the last few decades. According to folklore, the spring water is magical and is believed to particularly heal eye diseases. People used to take this healing water home, especially around the winter solstice. The water had to be drawn by a specific woman, referred to as the "pere emane" or family's female, in the local Sõrve dialect. In the past, people in Sõrve used the terms "emased" and "isased" for women and men, respectively.

locations.title: Allikasoo Bog
locations.description: Here is the highest area in Saaremaa, the Viidumägi Nature Reserve, where the Allikasoo Bog is located. The Allikasoo hiking trail leads to the bog, initially descending from up to an 18-meter escarpment. While hiking in Allikasoo, you will learn about the unique ecosystem it harbors and what sets it apart from regular bogs. You'll also get to see the Antsülusjärve beach ridge and the former sea bottom.

locations.title: Audaku Trail p1
locations.description: This point marks the farthest point of the Audaku hiking trail. The trail is located in the Viidumäe Nature Reserve and introduces the characteristic ecosystems and various forest types of the Western Saaremaa Highlands. The trail includes a boardwalk to cross a species-rich fen.

locations.title: Laialepa Bay
locations.description: Laialepa Bay divides the northern part of Harilaid into two narrow peninsulas. The lake is an old sea bay that was connected to the sea through Junkru Bay until it was filled by Soviet soldiers. The eastern shore of the lake is steep. The Harilaid hiking trail passes through this area.

locations.title: Ahisääre Lõugas
locations.description: The Harilaid hiking trail takes a circular route around Laialepa Bay and reaches Ahisääre Lõuka, where an interesting land formation, Ahisäär, is located. From here, you can enjoy a beautiful view of the sea and the surrounding nature.

locations.title: Nõgestelõugas
locations.description: The Harilaid hiking trail begins and ends close to the local Nõgestelõuka Lake.

locations.title: Kulpri Camping Ground
locations.description: The Kulpri camping area is located in the southwest part of Vilsandi Island. It offers a beautiful sandy beach with a view of the open sea and is known as a swimming spot for the island's residents. The camping area includes a table with benches, three additional benches, a covered fireplace, a dry toilet, and a shelter for firewood.

locations.title: Vilsandi Lighthouse
locations.description: Vilsandi Lighthouse is the westernmost lighthouse in Estonia, located on Saaremaa. The white stone tower, equipped with a lantern room and balcony, has a height of 37 meters from the base and a light elevation of 40 meters above sea level. The lighthouse was constructed in 1809 and operates year-round during the dark hours.

locations.title: Vesiloo Island
locations.description: Vesiloo Island is located north of Vilsandi Island in Saaremaa, approximately 500 meters away. The Vesiloo hiking trail leads to Vesiloo Island. The vegetation on Vesiloo is characterized by mountain current, juniper, and buckthorn. During low water levels, it is possible to walk from Vilsandi to Vesiloo through Riimisääre.

locations.title: Väike-Kaanda Lake
locations.description: Väike-Kaanda Lake is a small lake located at the farthest point of the Abula hiking trail, where the trail turns back towards the starting point.

locations.title: Kalasma Camping Ground
locations.description: Kalasma camping area is a beautiful seaside area covered with little vegetation and rocky terrain. It's a pleasant spot to enjoy sunsets and tranquility. The area features two picnic tables, a sheltered fireplace, two covered fire pits, a dry toilet, and a woodshed.

locations.title: Karjääri Camping Ground
locations.description: Karjääri camping area is a sheltered camping site located in a former gravel quarry that has now become a natural area. It is situated along the Abula-Kalasma study trail. Facilities include a covered fireplace, a picnic table, a woodshed, a dry toilet, and a bench.

locations.title: Abula Camping Ground
locations.description: Abula camping area is located on the beach with rocky coastlines of Tagamõisa Bay, stunted coastal pines, wind, sun, and a romantic atmosphere. The facilities include a covered fireplace, a roofed picnic table, a dry toilet, a shelter, an information board, and a woodshed.

locations.title: Rebastemäe Lookout Tower
locations.description: The Rebastemäe observation tower is located at the beginning of the Rebastemäe study trail in the oldest and highest part of Western Estonia, on the Kõpu Peninsula, on Rebastemägi. The tower offers views of the forests of Hiiumaa and Mardihansu Bay. You may also encounter wildlife along the trail, as Rebastemägi is named after the foxes that live in the sandy dunes here. On a clear day, the neighboring island of Saaremaa is visible.

locations.title: Kaplimäe Lookout Tower
locations.description: The Kaplimäe observation tower is located in the oldest and highest part of Western Estonia, on the Kõpu Peninsula, on Kaplimägi, which is the second-highest hill in Hiiumaa (63.5 m). The tower offers views of the forests of Hiiumaa, and to the north-east, the Baltic Sea. According to local folklore, there was once a chapel on the hill.

locations.title: Kärdla Rannamets
locations.description: The hiking trail created in the forests near Kärdla is suitable for both leisurely walks and enjoying nature. It is suitable for hiking in every season, but it should be noted that there are wetter and marshy areas on the trail. In dry and drought-like spring and summer conditions, it can be traversed with dry feet. However, during wetter seasons, it is advisable to wear waterproof footwear when venturing onto the trail.

locations.title: Leemeti Mäed
locations.description: The Leemeti hiking trail leads through the Leemeti forest. At this point, there are the Leemeti Hills, which add a lot to this beautiful landscape.

locations.title: Leemeti Forest
locations.description: The Leemeti hiking trail takes you through the Leemeti forest and there are two trails to take. At this point, the 2km long trail turns back towards the beginning.

locations.title: Rumpo Nina
locations.description: The Rumpo Peninsula, with its diverse density and age of juniper stands, is a unique habitat for rare lichen communities. It houses the only location in Estonia where the common tundra lichen grows. Please be cautious as these lichen areas are sensitive to trampling. The peninsula is used for grazing cattle, sheep, and horses.

locations.title: Suurallikas Spring
locations.description: Suurallikas is the largest among the numerous springs around the northern part of Lake Prästvigi. The summer flow rate of the spring is 10 liters per second, and it forms a pond with a depth of nearly half a meter. In the past, the spring was used to supply water to the manor.

locations.title: Prästvike Lake
locations.description: In the central part of Vormsi Island, there is Lake Prästvike, a former coastal lagoon that has largely become overgrown with reeds. The lake has retained its importance as a spawning and feeding area for coastal sea fish. The birdlife around the lake is abundant and diverse. On quiet spring evenings, you may hear the calls of various birds echoing in the area.

locations.title: Huitbergi Paekühm
locations.description: Huitberg Paekühm is a Limestone Hillock on Vormsi Island. Geologically, it is a coral reef over 400 million years old, smoothed by continental ice. On exposed surfaces, you can observe fossilized corals, stromatoporoids, trilobites, brachiopods, and bryozoans, among other features. The hillock runs in a north-south direction and is approximately 70 meters long, 20 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high.

locations.title: Marimetsa Bog
locations.description: Marimetsa Bog is a large bog in Lääne County. In Marimetsa Bog, you can observe various types of wetlands, including low bogs, transition bogs, and raised bogs. The area is rich in both fauna and flora. The bog started forming as mineral land turned into peatland after the retreat of Antsülus Lake. The thickness of the peat layer reaches up to 7 meters.

locations.title: Saare Lookout Tower 1
locations.description: The Saare Hiking Trail introduces Sutlepa Sea, which is a remnant lake separated from the sea due to land uplift. Along its shores, extensive reed fields stretch out. For a better view, two elevated viewing platforms have been constructed along the trail, providing a vantage point over the reeds and allowing birdwatching.

locations.title: Saare Lookout Tower 2
locations.description: The Saare Hiking Trail introduces Sutlepa Sea, which is a remnant lake separated from the sea due to land uplift. Along its shores, extensive reed fields stretch out. For a better view, two elevated viewing platforms have been constructed along the trail, providing a vantage point over the reeds and allowing birdwatching.

journeys.title: Paljassaare: West Side
journeys.description: The nature filled peninsula of Paljassaare is a great place for a day-trip. The peninsula can be divided into the west side and the east side. The west side you have a more secluded area with a wide choice of trails throughout it, with multiple former military structures to explore and a beautiful coastline to see.

journeys.title: Paljassaare: East Side
journeys.description: The nature filled peninsula of Paljassaare is a great place for a day-trip. The peninsula can be divided into the west side and the east side. The east side is a bit easier to get to than the west side and it includes great locations that have beautiful views over the plains or the sea.

journeys.title: The Remnants of Laagri
journeys.description: This journey will take you through the former military structures of Laagri. Laagri is a more widely known point of interest by the locals due to the tunnel system that is underneath the base. The base is not too big but it will take a bit to go through it all. The before mentioned tunnels are almost all filled with a significant amount of water but most of the tunnels can be traversed through using rubber boots

journeys.title: Astangu and the Depths
journeys.description: During the soviet times, Astangu had a military base which had ammo storages built into manmade tunnels, barracks and other similar military structures. Now the area is filled with these decaying structures along with multiple slightly dangerous tunnels and is slowly being taken back by nature.

journeys.title: A walk through Old Town
journeys.description: One of the most prevalant reasons for visiting Tallinn when it comes to tourists. There is a lot to see in the Old Town area, including bars, museums, sightseeing locations, restaurants etc but this journey will take you through the main sightseeing locations in the area for a basic overview.

journeys.title: The Greens of Männiku
journeys.description: Männiku is a subdistrict of Tallinn which has a lot of nature filled areas around. This is a nature oriented journey which will go through the Männiku Forest area and will continue to the Pääsküla Bog with a lot of nice sights on the way.

journeys.title: The Flow of Jägala
journeys.description: This journey takes place through locations which are located close to the Jägala river. The main attraction in the area is the Jägala waterfall which is a beautiful sight to see but the additional locations are also worth checking out.

journeys.title: Pärispea and It's Wonders
journeys.description: The peninsula of suurpea is a quiet area located a long way from any bigger city. For the most part it is covered in thick forests and winding roads from village to village. In addition to the peacefulness of the countryside, the peninsula has a lot of interesting locations to see, from abandoned military buildings to beatiful natural creations.

journeys.title: The Waters of Lahemaa
journeys.description: Quite a way from any bigger settlements, this journey will take you through the forests in the middle of the National Park of Lahemaa. Though the journey is a bit short, the locations are well worth the see, including both nature objects and also old man-made structures which are all located close to rivers.

journeys.title: Suurupi and the Remains
journeys.description: A journey showing you the many former military stuctures and other natural wonders in Suurupi. Not too far from the capital, the village of Suurupi has a lot of locations to see if you know where to look. The beautiful coastline and the remains of the naval defence bases are surely worth a day-trip.

journeys.title: The Lake of Klooga
journeys.description: The village of Klooga is situated next to the Klooga lake which is a rather big lake with a lot to see surprisingly. The lake itself is already worth a visit, especially if swimming is of interest, but once here the close by locations will show you some historic buildings, for example the former Military base here.

journeys.title: The Hidden Paldiski
journeys.description: In the previous Soviet era, a big part of Paldiski was habbited by a multitude of different military bases and settlements. Now the peninsula is mostly known for the city of Paldiski and the beautiful nature surrounding it. In addition to the nature scenery in the area, there are countless soviet time remains throughout the peninsula just waiting to be found.

journeys.title: Harku Forest Trail
journeys.description: This Journey takes place through the forest of Harku, which is the biggest forest within Tallinn. The trip has various trails to take and the landscape ranges from flatter fields to hill filled forest trails, a perfect forest experience.

journeys.title: Jussi Nature Trail
journeys.description: Explore the diverse and captivating landscapes of the Jussi Nature Trail, an eight-kilometer circular path within the North Kõrvemaa Nature Reserve in Estonia. Starting at the Koersilla parking lot near the Soodla River, with space for up to 20 cars or four buses, this trail is marked with yellow paint and takes you up and down along the Jussi Lakes. As you hike, you'll encounter various terrains, from heather-covered heathlands to rolling hills with diverse forest types and the enchanting Jussi lake landscape. The highlight of this journey is the unique heathland, making the Jussi Nature Trail a must-visit for nature enthusiasts exploring Estonia.

journeys.title: Sõõriksoo Nature Trail
journeys.description:
Aegviidu, nestled between two protected areas, Põhja-Kõrvemaa Nature Reserve and Kõrvemaa Landscape Protection Area, is an ideal destination for nature enthusiasts. Starting from the heart of Aegviidu, at the RMK Aegviidu Nature Center (located in the former Aegviidu Forest District building), you'll find the short and informative Sõõriksoo Nature Trail (1.5 km).
The Sõõriksoo Trail is well-equipped with information and marked clearly with yellow ribbons on trees and signposts, making it easy to navigate without a guide. Along the trail, you'll discover 15 information boards highlighting key points of interest.
In the past, the term "Sõõriksoo" referred to the Aegviidu side of Rehessaare bog, where a significant peat industry operated in the early 20th century. Today, the remnants of the peat industry, such as peat ditches interspersed with elevated ridges and haulage roads, remain distinguishable in the natural landscape. Over time, the bog's vegetation has made a remarkable recovery, featuring species like cranberries, bog rosemary, cottongrass, white beak-rush, bog bilberry, and other characteristic bog plants.

journeys.title: Paukjärve Nature Trail
journeys.description:
The Paukjärve Nature Trail, located in the North Kõrvemaa Nature Reserve, offers a five-kilometer loop through captivating landscapes. The trail begins at Kaksiksilla near the Soodla River and boasts eleven points of interest, including four stunning lakes: Paukjärv, Linajärv, Kaasikjärv, and the deepest in Northern Estonia, Umerikjärv (14 m). A picturesque observation tower between the Great Bog and Paukjärv provides breathtaking views, considered among Kõrvemaa's finest.
Marked with yellow paint, the trail takes approximately two hours to complete and showcases the beauty of Paukjärv, one of Estonia's clearest lakes with water transparency of up to six meters. Its low nutrient levels result in minimal aquatic vegetation, featuring protected species like the floating bur-reed and water lobelia. The surrounding pristine nature has been a source of inspiration for renowned author A. H. Tammsaare. Alongside the exquisite lakes, the trail reveals intriguing landforms formed during the melting of continental ice, characteristic of North Kõrvemaa. Explore this remarkable journey through nature and history.

journeys.title: Majakivi Nature Trail
journeys.description:
Explore the unique Juminda Peninsula Nature Trail, which showcases a diverse landscape with sand dunes, forested ridges, and wetlands. You can start your adventure from either the Hara–Juminda road parking lot or the Aabla–Tapurla road parking lot, though the latter can be challenging during rainy periods.
The 6.1-kilometer trail, marked with white-green signs on trees, offers various points of interest, including the impressive Majakivi erratic boulder. At 13.5 meters high, the Pikanõmme observation tower provides stunning views of the peninsula, Kolga Bay, and even distant Tallinn.
You'll encounter traces of ancient shorelines, diverse wetlands, and glacial features, with the landscape shaped by retreating continental ice. The area is known for its stony elevations and scattered glacial erratics, like Majakivi, which you can climb via a ladder. This nature trail is a compact journey through history and unique natural landscapes.

journeys.title: Paunküla Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Paunküla mountain hiking trails are situated in the Paunküla landscape protection area, with a combined length of 12 kilometers. The trails begin at the Rõõsa Lake parking area, along the Tallinn-Tartu highway, with space for several cars or buses. The trails diverge at Suur-Kaksjärve, 1.5 kilometers from the start. These marked paths lead hikers to various lakes, including Suur-Kaksjärve, Väike-Kaksjärve, Rahkjärve, Lindjärve, Mustjärve, and Punajärve, amidst a landscape of hills, ridges, bogs, and open areas. This diversity is complemented by a range of forest types and tree species, suitable for hikers of all ages. Along the Suur-Kaksjärve, there is also a designated campfire site for resting, cooking, and even camping.

journeys.title: Keila-Joa Nature Trail
journeys.description: The Keila-Joa Park Nature Trail is a 2.3 km path located in Keila-Joa, Harju County, Estonia. The trail starts from the Keila-Joa Park parking lot, running along the Keila River. It takes approximately 1-1.5 hours to complete the trail. Along the path, you'll find 9 information boards describing interesting natural features and the history of Keila-Joa Park. The highlight of the trail, regardless of the season, is the stunning Keila Waterfall. Downstream from the waterfall, you'll encounter high, forest-covered banks that offer picturesque natural views. The park's unique character is largely owed to the work of gardener Friedrich Winkler. The left bank of the river transitioned into a natural park over time, while the complex system of meadows on the right bank has evolved into a central meadow. Many introduced tree species have been replaced by native ones, and the purposeful tree groups that once shaped the park's design, like spruces and oaks, are now barely noticeable. The riverbank and the distinctive river islands are covered by alder buckthorn floodplain forests.

journeys.title: Valgejärve Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Valgejärve Nature Trail starts and ends at the Järveotsa Lake, an RMK camping site where you can also have a campfire. It spans 6.5 km and takes approximately 2 hours to complete. Along the trail, informative boards provide insights into the formation of Valgejärv and the different forest types in the area, including heath forests, spruce forests, broad-leaved forests, swamp forests, transition mires, and bog forests.
The unique Valgejärv, a lake with a three-meter-thick, distinctive marl covering, is accessed from the Järveotsa Lake, where a marked forest path stretches for 2 km. At the shore of Valgejärv, you'll find a small shelter for resting and learning about the historical use of marl from the lake. Following this, a 1.2 km long boardwalk leads you to an observation tower overlooking the lake. From this six-meter-high tower, you can enjoy a splendid view of the marl-covered eastern shore of the lake and the inflowing streams. The nature trail then continues across the sand dunes to Pisuksenõmme Road. Several springs that feed the lake can be found along the eastern shore of Valgejärv. Signposts direct you to one of these springs, situated 50 meters off the trail in the midst of the bog, where you can refill your water bottle before returning to Järveotsa.
Valgejärv gets its name from the unique marl layer covering the lake's bottom, which is up to three meters thick. This marl has formed and continues to develop from the marl-rich substrate of the lake and the inflow of springs.

journeys.title: Neeruti Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Neeruti nature trail offers two options for hikers, with 1.5 km and 3.5 km circular routes. The trail runs along the ridges of long northwest-southeast-oriented hills, some of which have steep slopes reaching up to 45 degrees. The shorter route encircles Tagajärv Lake and takes you to Sadulamägi, an ancient hillfort on the southwestern shore of Eesjärv Lake. The longer route extends to Kiissaaugumägi.
The trail follows the ridges of hills formed around 10,000 years ago.

journeys.title: Äntu Nature Trail
journeys.description:
The approximately 10 km long nature trail in the Äntu Landscape Reserve offers a diverse landscape and rich natural surroundings. The trail passes through various types of forests and the steep Punamäe hill, where the largest castle in Southern Virumaa, Agelinde, was located. Along the trail, you'll encounter seven different small lakes: Sinijärv, Vahe- or Roheline järv, Valgejärv, Linaleo järv, Mäetaguse järv, Kaanjärv, and Umbjärv – true gems of this region with their clear and emerald-blue waters, a rarity in the Baltics. 
The trail is marked with directional signs and equipped with information boards.

journeys.title: Oandu-Võsu Hiking Trail
journeys.description: The Oandu-Võsu hiking trail covers 9.5 kilometers and is suitable for walking, cycling, and skiing in winter. It takes approximately 4 hours to complete without stops and is marked with red dots or ribbons on trees, signs at intersections, and information is available at the RMK Oandu Nature Center. The trail begins near the Oandu Nature Center, descending from the 10-meter Koljaku-Oandu ridge. It mostly follows the ancient Seljaku road through pristine forests, passing numerous springs, streams, and ditches that feed from the Koljaku-Oandu ridge. The trail is surrounded by old-growth forests protected since the establishment of Lahemaa National Park in 1971, providing a glimpse of primeval woodlands. Additionally, some non-native tree species like white pine, spruce, and mountain pine were planted here during the estate era. Towards the Võsu end, forest-covered dunes, known locally as "Sae mäed," rise to nearly 10 meters in height, featuring heritage objects along their slopes, including boundary stones, old tar oven ruins, and remnants of a mill dam along the Võsu River.

journeys.title: Viitna Nature Trail
journeys.description: Embark on the captivating Viitna Nature Trail, a 7-kilometer path in Estonia's Viitna Landscape Protection Area. Enjoy breathtaking views of the "lake mountains" and explore geological formations shaped by the ice age. The trail introduces you to three Viitna lakes, with Pikkjärv being of particular ecological significance. With 27 points of interest, the hike takes 3-4 hours but offers a shorter 2.5-kilometer loop for those with limited time. Exercise caution during rainy weather, especially near Nabudi lake. Explore the geological wonders of Viitna on this educational and scenic nature trail.

journeys.title: Oandu Nature Trail
journeys.description: The Oandu Nature Trail, located in Lahemaa National Park, near Oandu, is a 4.6-kilometer circular trail that starts at the Koljaku-Sagadi-Altja crossroads and is also accessible from the RMK Oandu Nature Center. It's a pedestrian-only trail, with about half of it covered by boardwalks. Along this trail, visitors can explore pristine forests where human interference with natural development has been minimal. Fallen trees and stumps provide habitats for rare fungi, mosses, lichens, and insects. Springs flow amid ferns, and the paths of forest animals wind through the landscape. The trail features 19 points of interest, showcasing the development of natural forests, various species, landforms, and traces of wildlife activities. You can complete the trail in approximately 2 hours.

journeys.title: Käsmu Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Explore Käsmu, the smallest peninsula in Lahemaa, known for its captivating captain's village, historic maritime school, lush pine forests, rocky shores, and intriguing landmarks. Discover this natural wonderland on foot or by bike along well-marked trails. Please follow the green-white markings for the nature trail.
The Käsmu hiking trail, marked in red, spans 15 kilometers in a loop around the peninsula, with nearly 10 kilometers hugging the coastline. It overlaps with other paths throughout the village but continues along the sea and forest border in the Madlilepa lõuka area. The trail leads to the kivikülv (stone field) on your way to Palganeem, which is about 3.5 kilometers from the rocky beach. Palganeem is home to a Soviet border guard searchlight tower as a lasting relic. Hike along the western coastline of the peninsula, where the forest-covered rocky slopes rise steeply in some areas. If you take the blue-marked cycling path from Tiirneem to Keripõllu, you can shorten your hike by 2.5 kilometers, but you will miss the Käsmu Lake and the high sandy ridge called Järvekael. The parking lot near the lake's shore marks the farthest point of the trail, which then loops back to reach Käsmu village after a 4.5-kilometer walk along Keripõllu Road.

journeys.title: Altja Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Discover Altja Nature Trail, a 3-kilometer journey through the historic fishing village of Altja, surrounded by coastal pine forests and rocky shores. The well-marked trail begins in the village center, near the tavern, with white-green tree signs guiding the way.
Along the route, you'll encounter historical landmarks like the 19th-century Mäekõrts (Altja tavern) and Toomarahva farmhouse, now a tourist accommodation. The path leads to Kiigemäe, where a restored swing offers a glimpse into coastal community life.
You'll traverse a dense pine forest with ancient trees, cross the Altja River via Kärbse palgi, and explore the coastline with a glimpse of the former Soviet-restricted coastal border. The trail passes Laiakari lumber yard and offers a view of Merinõmme community hall ruins.
You can briefly detour to Käbinõmme summerhouse, a well-preserved 19th-century estate, and explore Altja Peninsula, rich in glacial erratics. The trail completes its loop through the village, providing a compact experience of Altja's history and natural beauty.

journeys.title: Seljamäe Study Trail
journeys.description:
The Seljamäe Nature Study and Hiking Trail forms a loop around the southern part of Punasoo, which is part of the larger Tudu bog complex, stretching about 20 km from north to south and averaging 4 km in width. The Tudu bog complex developed as a result of the former lowland lakes and spring-fed areas getting submerged by peat. 
The marked hiking trail starts from here, taking you to the circular 5 km long nature study trail in Seljamäe.
Seljamägi is a hill where the northern part is lower, and the southern part is higher, with elevations reaching up to 91 meters. Some areas have steep slopes, with heights of up to 10 meters.. The dominant forest type in Seljamäe is mixed forests.
The name "Punasoo" comes from a local legend that says Kalevipoeg, a legendary figure in Estonian folklore, plowed up the neighboring Sirtsi bog. After completing the work, he left his horse to rest. During that time, wolves attacked the horse and killed it in the area now known as Punasoo. The name "Punasoo" is said to come from the blood of the slain horse.

journeys.title: Kuimetsa Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
In one of Estonia's largest and most unique karst areas, you can witness various karst formations, including sinkholes, funnels, cavities, channels, and caves. Five caves are so extensive that you can easily walk inside. One of the most impressive caves is a chamber-like cavern that measures 12 meters in length and 8 meters in width. During the high-water season, you can observe water flowing in many of the cracks and channels, but during low-water periods, the water flow is nearly invisible.
These karst formations are part of Estonia's largest karst caves.

journeys.title: Loosalu Bog Nature Trail
journeys.description: The nature trail is 7.7 kilometers long, with 4 kilometers of boardwalks following reconstruction. It's not a circular trail, so for a round trip, you should be prepared for approximately 15.4 kilometers. Starting from Loosalu, the boardwalk begins at the edge of the bog, passes Loosalu Lake, and traverses the bog and forest until you reach the first farmstead. From there, the path ascends along forest and field paths to the foothills of Paluküla Hiiemägi.

journeys.title: Salevere Hiking Trail
journeys.description: A 1.5 km trail winds along an esker, which sits about a dozen meters above the surrounding terrain. The esker is covered with diverse mixed forest, while below, you can observe the relatively rare hornbeam forest in Estonia. The beginning of the trail passes through a juniper grove, in the middle, you can take a detour to see the remains of an ancient defensive structure's wall, and as you reach the end of the trail, you can enjoy refreshing spring water.

journeys.title: Penijõe Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Matsalu National Park trail offers various options to explore the characteristic landscapes of Matsalu (reeds, seasonal floodplain meadows, and meadows) and get to know the local wildlife. Along the trail, there is an 8-meter-high observation tower located on the edge of the reeds.
The hiking trail can be completed in four different ways, with lengths ranging from 3.2 km (to and from the observation tower), 4.7 km, 6 km, or 7 km (for circular routes). The trail traverses partially grazed areas and consists mainly of dirt paths. It is marked with wooden directional signs.

journeys.title: Kaisma Suurjärve Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The hiking trail is 6 km long, with approximately 1.5 km of the trail covered with planks to ensure comfortable walking and prevent soil damage. You'll find stately pine trees, likely serving as bird courting grounds for centuries. If you look closely, you might discover claw marks on the tree trunks, a sign that there might be bears.
This trail offers diverse and ever-changing landscapes. It takes you into a bog with a scenic viewpoint tower that provides a beautiful view of Kaisma bog. Near the trail, you may notice drainage ditches that, had they been connected to the lake, would have dramatically transformed the natural environment.

journeys.title: Riisa Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Riisa educational trail is a 4.8 km long boardwalk located near Kõpu-Jõesuu road, offering easy access to hikers. It winds through a picturesque bog and the Navesti River's forested areas, with platforms along the way for resting and a viewing tower. The boardwalk features informative signs.
The trail traverses a raised bog, a type of bog that is exclusively rain-fed and accumulates peat. These bogs rise above the water levels of their surroundings, giving rise to a distinct landscape. The bog ecosystems have relatively low species diversity, featuring plants like peat mosses, cranberries, and heather.
The educational trail also follows part of the Navesti River's bank, which is one of Estonia's most water-rich rivers, ultimately flowing into the Pärnu River.

journeys.title: Meiekose Study Trail
journeys.description:
The Meiekose educational trail starts at the visitor center, about one kilometer towards Jõesuu, near the border of Viljandi and Pärnu counties. This 2.8 km trail leads you along the former Kõpu-Jõesuu railway dam, offering insights into the Tõramaa River estuary meadows, a part of the Soomaa heritage landscapes.
The trail showcases wetlands, various plant species, beautiful river bends, and majestic oak trees. It can be traversed on foot, by bike, or horseback and is suitable for cross-country skiing in the winter.
You can start your journey from either the Tõramaa or Meiekose end. The trail includes side paths leading to old farmsteads, Lilleniit, Tammeraja, and Metsaheinamaa. Informative signs are placed along the way.
Lilleniit, a small meadow, boasts various rare plant species best observed during the peak flowering season in July. It's important to note that picking protected plants is not allowed.
Opting for the Tammering detour will extend your hike by about 0.5 km. Along the riverbank meadows, you'll encounter grand oak trees, some of which are over three hundred years old.
The area around the Raudna and Tõramaa River estuary features picturesque and diverse laminiit meadows, making it an ideal spot to rest, relax, and even have a campfire.

journeys.title: Kõveri Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Starting from Kõveri Recreation Center, a marked and information-signposted hiking trail leads through areas where lime burning took place in the past and to the beginning of Timmkanal.
The trail follows the scenic banks of the Ura River, adorned with ferns and rare laminiit forests. It passes by the remains of the former Kõveri large farmstead, marked by cellar ruins and old trees. The trail is approximately 5 km long, taking about 2 hours to complete.
Upon reaching the Pärapõrgu recreation site, you'll have covered over half of the hiking trail. Here, hikers can light a campfire and enjoy a meal.

journeys.title: Laiksaare Nature Trail
journeys.description: The Laiksaare nature trail is located in Saarde Parish, in the village of Laiksaare, spanning a length of 1.5 km. The trail begins at the RMK Pärnu County Forest Office building, where there is a parking lot and an information board with a map of the trail. To leisurely complete the nature trail, you should allow 1 to 1.5 hours. Along the trail, you will come across 3 footbridges, several points of interest, and the Laiksaare forest cabin with a fireplace. The trail winds through a species-rich and diverse Rannametsa River floodplain forest where the trees are of substantial size. The unique feature of this trail is the infrequently seen floodplain forests in Estonia and the plant communities associated with them.

journeys.title: Tolkuse Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The trail begins at the Rannametsa paved parking lot near an information board.
This circular 2.2 km educational trail offers an opportunity to explore one of the most beautiful and tallest coastal pine forests in Estonia, as well as the unique Tolkuse bog, which formed as a result of the swampy conditions between sand dunes. In the central part of the bog, you'll find dozens of larger and smaller ponds, known as "laukaid" or lakes, according to local naming. Tolkuse bog is one of the few lagoon-type bogs that has reached the bog stage both in Estonia and in Europe. A spectacular view of Tolkuse bog can be enjoyed from the Tornimäe observation tower (18 m) located on the Tornimäe hill. This tower was initially erected for cartographic purposes as early as 1904. Combined with the 34 m high dune ridge at Tornimäe, it offers an observation point more than fifty meters above sea level.
The trail leads around the largest kettle lake in the bog, measuring approximately 96x210 meters. The lakes and kettles in the central part of the bog host plants indicative of nutrient-rich waters, such as hundinui, mürkputk, ubaleht, along with typical species for low-lying wetlands, like tarna and ferns, and even some stunted spruce trees.
The main value of the educational trail is the opportunity to become acquainted with the rare bog ecosystem that formed as a result of the lagoon becoming swampy amid coastal dunes.

journeys.title: Kabli Nature Trail
journeys.description:
Kabli Nature Education Trail is approximately 2 km long. The trail starts from Kabli Nature Center and follows the coastline southward. You can also access the trail from Kabli Bird Station. Information about the local nature can be obtained from the observation points along the trail, as well as from information boards. The trail is marked with directional signs to help you navigate its twists and turns.
The primary goal of the trail is to introduce visitors to the natural beauty of the coastal area of Häädemeeste and to educate them about the decades of bird migration research conducted at Kabli Bird Station. The unique features of the trail include Kabli's sandy seashore, dunes, coastal meadows, and coniferous forests, all contributing to a rich and diverse network of habitats.

journeys.title: Nigula Bog Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Nigula Bog is located within the Nigula Nature Reserve, which has been protected since 1957. The bog formed as a result of lake overgrowth and is primarily characterized as a raised bog. Nigula Marsh features bog islands, known as "peaks," which are the higher areas between ancient lakes.
To the west of the bog, you'll find the relict lake Nigula Lake. A 6 km round trip educational trail begins from its shores and runs along the raised bog until it reaches the largest bog island, known as Salupeaks. The deciduous forest on this island is a remnant of a warmer and wetter climate that existed 5,000 to 6,000 years ago. Along the trail, you'll also encounter a viewing tower and a low platform, perfect for observing the activities of the bog's inhabitants.

journeys.title: Sandra Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Sookuninga Nature Reserve is located near the southern border of Pärnu County and is partially shared with Latvia. Within this nature reserve, you can find the small 400 m Sandra hiking trail in the Rongu Bog. This trail offers a fantastic view of the bog landscape. At the beginning of the trail, there is a forest hut where you can rest or even stay overnight for free. The resting place also includes a covered fireplace with a grill grate for cooking.
Due to challenging road conditions, it is recommended to leave your car by the roadside before the last kilometer and continue on foot. Always stick to the marked trails as the bog is a delicate ecosystem.

journeys.title: Lilli Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Lilli hiking trail is located in Lilli village, Viljandi County, and starts from in front of the Lilli Nature House. This 2.9 km long trail is designed to introduce visitors to the nature surrounding the Nature House. It mainly passes through the Alliku nature reserve (88 hectares), which is established for the preservation of established or developing natural habitats. The trail follows old forest roads, paths, and currently used forest roads.

One of the farthest and more remarkable locations along the trail is a rowan grove with a valuable habitat for the protected lungwort lichen. There is also a unique boulder hugged by a rowan and two spruce trees with their roots. This stone has been used as a place for offerings. When passing through the shooting range belonging to the Ministry of Defense, be cautious. Please read the information provided on the information boards about when it is safe to enter this area.

Be sure to take a walk through the forest road to the loaf-shaped Virapuu glacial erratic. The trail can be completed in different sections:

The entire trail: Nature House – Lime tree alley – Rowan grove – Lungwort lichen – Badger burrows – Old nesting tree of the black stork – Feeding trough – Virapuu glacial erratic – Nature House.
Rowan trail: Nature House – Lime tree alley – Rowan grove – Lungwort lichen – Lime tree alley – Nature House.
Badger trail: Nature House – Lime tree alley – Badger burrows – Old nesting tree of the black stork – Feeding trough – Virapuu glacial erratic – Nature House.
Black Stork trail: Nature House – Shooting range path – Old nesting tree of the black stork – Feeding trough – Virapuu glacial erratic – Nature House.
The trail features information boards describing plants and animals at various points of interest, and it is marked with wooden directional signs to help visitors navigate through different sections of the trail.

journeys.title: Teringi Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Teringi Nature Trail is located in Viljandi County, near the Latvian border, on the state forest land of RMK Sakala recreation area. The trail forms a loop around the Teringi Landscape Protection Area, situated in the northern part of the protected area. The protection area was established in 1999 with the goal of conserving diverse swamp and forest landscapes and associated plant communities.
Due to its location near the Estonian-Latvian border, the area remains untouched by human activity, and its forests exhibit several characteristic features of primeval forests. Teringi Swamp and the surrounding forest areas serve as breeding grounds for the Eurasian crane (sookurg) and capercaillie (metsis). The protection area is bordered to the east by the Suuga-Putniku Ridge, which is 4-5 kilometers wide and has an absolute height of up to 122.5 meters. This ridge offers a beautiful view of Teringi Swamp, the shimmering Alatsi (Nava) Lake in the southeast of the brown-water swamp, and the winding boardwalk in the swamp. In the swamp, there are several islands, which used to be connected by a winter road to the southern Lotus Farm. 
Teringi Nature Trail consists of a 4.5 km long circular boardwalk. To reach the beginning of the trail, RMK Teringi campfire site, drive 1.5 km towards Peraküla from the center of Lilli village. At the start of the trail, facilities for hikers include a parking lot, toilet, campfire site, and a shelter. The trail features numerous information stands that introduce different forest types, flora, fauna, and avifauna. A reasonable time for a nature observation and impressions-recording hike is about 2-3 hours (you can complete the trail without breaks in about two hours). There are several resting places along the trail, and a swimming area is created by the shore of Alatsi Bog Lake.
The most captivating aspects of the nature trail include diverse forests, abundant plant life, and the mysterious Alatsi Lake.

journeys.title: Õisu Hiking Trail
journeys.description: The hiking trail is located near Õisu Manor, a little north of it, along the picturesque banks of Vidava Stream. Near the spacious parking lot, an information sign guides visitors through the gate onto a diverse trail that offers beautiful scenery and requires moderate physical effort. The stream with its rapids provides visual beauty and the soothing sound of running water all year round. A careful observer may even find the traces of a virgin water carrier on one of the outcrops. The Õisu River Valley is also known as the "Taevaskoda of Mulgimaa.”

journeys.title: Öördi Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Öördi Bog, located in Soomaa National Park, is unique among the park's bogs as it retains remnants of a once large glacial lake along its edge. The thickness of peat layers in the bog reaches up to 9.5 meters. The bog originated from the gradual transformation of the former lake into a peatland, with the surrounding low-lying area turning into mineral soil. The central part of the bog is characterized by numerous small hummocks in a pattern of ridge and hollow formations. The southern part is comparatively drier, with sporadic hummocks, transitioning into wet pine forests. A narrow and wet lichen zone separates the central and southern massifs. The central part of the bog supports a diverse range of mire plant species, indicating groundwater inflow through the peat. The presence of reed beds and hummocks is characteristic of the bog's margins, which have been preserved due to the absence of drainage elsewhere. In the eastern part of Öördi Bog, Öördi Lake, covering an area of 4.4 hectares, is found. The outflowing ditch from the lake has likely affected the water level of the lake, and remnants of the former lake shore are still visible. This area is known for having one of the most abundant cranberry populations in Estonia.

journeys.title: Ingatsi Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Ingatsi Nature Trail is a 3 km long trail that begins in Karuskose and follows a forest path to a high point on the Kuresoo bog's edge, which happens to be the highest bog ridge in Europe at 8 meters above sea level. From the observation tower on the bog's edge, visitors can enjoy a beautiful view of the bog and the Toonoja forest beyond. The trail then meanders between picturesque hummocks, takes a short loop through the bog, and guides hikers back along the same route. You should plan for about 1.5 hours for this hike. Along the Ingatsi trail, there are several rest areas and benches where you can take a break, stretch your legs, and even enjoy a refreshing dip in one of the bog's pools.

journeys.title: Lemmjõe Study Trail
journeys.description:
The 5.8km trail follows the banks of the Raudna River up to the confluence of Raudna and Lemmjõe. From there, it returns along the banks of the Lemmjõe River and ends back at Kuusekäära. The trail is a ground path and can be submerged during high tide, making it impassable. Along the Raudna River, there's also a designated area for making a fire and setting up a tent.
Lemmjõe Keelemets is a floodplain forest that covers 60 hectares between the Raudna and Lemmjõe rivers. A floodplain forest refers to a forest growing on a river's floodplain, which can be inundated by the river. Floods shape the appearance and characteristics of such forests. Thousands of years ago, floodplain forests were widespread in Estonia, consisting of broad-leaved trees like oaks, sycamores, ash, and hornbeam.
As you move away from the Raudna River, the floodplain forest transitions into a swamp forest. At this point, the grassy fringe becomes less abundant, and the moss carpet becomes denser. Trees grow on hummocks, mainly silver birch and aspen. The shrub layer includes rowan and common alder buckthorn. In the grassy fringe, you'll find both forest and swamp plants. Forest plants mainly grow on hummocks, while swamp plants are found in the hollows.

journeys.title: Kuuraniidu Study Trail
journeys.description:
The trail has a length of 1.8 km and takes you through an old spruce swamp forest, forming a circular path back to the starting point. Notable features on the trail include large and majestic spruce trees, which have been weathered and damaged in places over time and by storms. At the beginning of the trail, there is a designated area for making fires and camping.
Kuuraniidu Forest is a spruce swamp forest that has emerged as a result of drainage. Typical of this type of forest are frequent windthrow events. The trunks of spruce trees often lie on the ground, with high root walls standing upright. Birch trees also grow abundantly in this forest, approaching an age of a hundred years. Shade-tolerant plants like wood horsetail, marsh fern, lily of the valley, and barren strawberry can be found on the forest floor. Ferns are also common. In more well-lit clearings, blueberries and lingonberries grow. Kuuraniidu Forest is rich in fungi.
Please be cautious of slippery conditions on the trail during rainy weather and the potential danger of falling trees during strong winds.

journeys.title: Tõramaa Beaver Trail
journeys.description:
The Beaver Trail, or "Koprarada," commences near the Soomaa National Park Nature Center, offering year-round accessibility by road. During winter and spring floods, a substantial portion of the boardwalk may be submerged. This 1.8 km looped hiking trail is covered with wood chips, with boardwalks in the wettest areas. The trail guides you through various forest types, including dim spruce groves, open fern-covered birch forests, and the damp deciduous woods along the Mardu River, where a beaver family has settled. Here, you can closely inspect traces of their activity.
As you proceed along the riverbank, information boards point out interesting natural features and the beavers' architectural achievements. Looking over the river offers a beautiful view of a landscape that is becoming increasingly rare – a meadow marsh, particularly delightful in the spring with vibrant wildflower blooms. Tree trunks and shrubs host various mushroom fruiting bodies, lichens, and other intriguing elements. The path along the river is lined with felled trees and stumps, resembling sharpened pencil tips. Everywhere, you'll see trees marked with bite traces on their bark. Piles of wood chips lie beneath some trees, while larger trees are stripped on-site, and branches and smaller items are transported to their feeding dens.

journeys.title: Sakala Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Sakala Hiking Trail begins at the Vanaõue Recreation Center (located along the Vändra-Mudiste highway, near Navesti Heath landscape protection area) and ends near the Kurgja - Carl Robert Jakobson Museum. This 12 km hiking trail is marked with yellow-red stripes on wooden posts and kilometer markers. The trail follows forest roads and paths, with bridges built to cross ditches. During the spring to fall season, parts of the trail may be wet. There are five resting places along the trail (Vanaõue, Luidete, Maltssaare, Kabelimäe, and Mullasamba). If you start from Vanaõue, you'll find the Saeveski forest cabin after 8 km, where you can make a fire and even spend the night. The trail is also suitable for cycling and skiing in the winter.
The Sakala Tee Hiking Trail introduces the journey that Carl Robert Jakobson made when traveling from Kurgja to Viljandi to work on the "Sakala" newspaper. The points of interest along the trail introduce the rich natural surroundings, historical, and cultural heritage of the area. It takes approximately 3-4 hours to complete the trail.
One of the key features of the Sakala Tee Hiking Trail is the opportunity to explore the famous forests of the Vändra region. Information boards along the trail provide insights into the local flora, trees and shrubs, rare meadows, and old farm and forest guard sites.

journeys.title: Hüpassaare Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The educational trail has a total length of approximately 4.4 km, with one-third of it being covered with wood chips and the remaining two-thirds consisting of a boardwalk. The initial part of the trail extends through a kõdusoomets, which is a type of forest formed due to drainage of swampy areas. As you continue, the terrain gradually rises, and with the appearance of peat moss mats, the forest vegetation recedes. The ground is then covered with plants like bilberry, heather, bog bilberry, and other marsh plants, with mainly pine trees remaining. You've reached the edge of Kuresoo Bog.
Kuresoo is the largest of the Soomaa bogs, covering an area of 11,000 hectares in the eastern part of the Pärnu lowland. The southern part of the bog is also known as Suitsna, while the northern part goes by the name Leetva Bog. The Soomaa landscape in Kuresoo is diverse, featuring ridges, hollows, bog funnels, hidden brooks, and streams that drain water from the bog. The bog is a very wet area, where parts of plant residues do not decompose and instead accumulate as peat. Typically, peat mosses, cotton grasses, and reeds are responsible for peat formation.

journeys.title: Parika Väikejärve Hiking Trail
journeys.description: The educational trail is known for its heart-shaped bog lake. Aerial photographs of the lake adorn many publications about Viljandimaa. The trail is great for groups, as the wood chip path leading to the lake is sufficiently wide. Additionally, the wood chip path is suitable for people with mobility issues. The area around the trail features abundant blueberry pine forests, and further in the bog, you can find bilberries and cranberries. You also have the opportunity to take a dip in the rejuvenating bog lake.

journeys.title: Heimtali Study Trail
journeys.description:
The educational trail complements the Heimtali manor complex wonderfully. To reach the trail, drive from the heart of Heimtali Manor towards Viljandi. You can find a parking lot by the enigmatic little house resembling a gnome's house, which is actually a distillery.
Heimtali educational trail is a 2.5 km long path that passes by Estonia's only Heimtali lime tree grove. The trail is suitable for nature enthusiasts who are enchanted by majestic broadleaf trees. The trunks of these giant trees are covered with various rare mosses, many of which are indicator species for valuable habitats.

journeys.title: Soontaga Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Soontaga hiking trail with a length of 2.9km passes through a mixed forest typical of southern Estonia, reaching the banks of the Small Emajõgi River. Along the hiking trail, you'll find several information boards that introduce the local nature of the area.

journeys.title: Pühajärve Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The hiking trail around Lake Pühajärv starts from the Otepää Nature Park Center. This 13 km long trail takes you on an adventure around one of Estonia's unique and beautiful lakes.
Key natural sites along the trail: Lake Pühajärv, Armuallikas (Spring of Love), the source of the Small Emajõgi River, Koolirand (School Beach), Murrumets (Shattered Forest), the War Oak of Lake Pühajärv, and the War Oak Forest.
It usually takes an average of 3-6 hours to complete the trail. Along the route, there are rest and campfire sites. Part of the trail (approximately 2.2 km) runs along the Pühajärve-Kääriku sports track, next to the Otepää-Kääriku road.
The trail is suitable for both hiking and cycling, but cyclists should be prepared for sections where they might need to push their bikes. RMK Kiigemäe and Kooliranna campfire sites are also located along the hiking trail, where camping and making fires are allowed.

journeys.title: Murrumetsa Hiking Trail
journeys.description: The circular Murrumetsa trail runs through Pühajärve Park's Murrumetsa and back. The trail is 3.5 km long and takes about 1-1.5 hours to complete. It's recommended to walk this trail. The trail starts from the Pühajärve Recreation Center's parking lot, next to the Otepää-Kääriku highway. Along the trail, informative boards with texts in Estonian, English, and Finnish provide insights into the local Otepää forest, wildlife, and plant life within the cultural landscape.

journeys.title: Taagepera- Ala Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Taagepera hiking trail has a total length of 5.4 km. The trail starts and ends at the Taagepera campfire site, where you can camp and make a fire. Along the Õhne River's banks, you can see signs of beaver activity, forest grouse, and a diverse forest with species such as spruce, pine, oak, and more. You can also choose to hike smaller sections of the trail with lengths of 1.6 km and 4.0 km.
The main attractions on this hiking trail include Taagepera Castle with its castle park, Taagepera Church and cemetery, the baronial family cemetery, "Musukivi" stone, a sacrificial stone, an underground cemetery, and the Õhne River.

journeys.title: Jalase Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Jalase Nature Trail is 2 km in length. Camping and making fires are not allowed by the lake or on the nature trail.
The Jalase Landscape Protection Area aims to protect various ecosystems, rare and endangered species, geological features, and the unique cultural landscape of Jalase. There are three lekking grounds for the capercaillie within the protection area.
The nature trail begins at a rest area located on the Jalase-Ohukotsu road. Initially, it goes through a 1 km section of the Arbumetsa protected area, showcasing a diverse forest. Then, it follows the edge of a field for 300 meters before transitioning into a 0.7 km long boardwalk. The boardwalk leads through the Sõbesoo protected area, taking visitors to Sõbessoo or Jalase Lake. It's recommended for hikers to return to the starting point along the same path from the lake.

journeys.title: Mukri Nature Trail
journeys.description:
The Jalase Nature Trail is 2 km in length. Camping and making fires are not allowed by the lake or on the nature trail.
The Jalase Landscape Protection Area aims to protect various ecosystems, rare and endangered species, geological features, and the unique cultural landscape of Jalase. There are three lekking grounds for the capercaillie within the protection area.
The nature trail begins at a rest area located on the Jalase-Ohukotsu road. Initially, it goes through a 1 km section of the Arbumetsa protected area, showcasing a diverse forest. Then, it follows the edge of a field for 300 meters before transitioning into a 0.7 km long boardwalk. The boardwalk leads through the Sõbesoo protected area, taking visitors to Sõbessoo or Jalase Lake. It's recommended for hikers to return to the starting point along the same path from the lake.

journeys.title: Sopa Spring Hiking Trail
journeys.description: If you walk to Sopa Spring and back, you will cover a trail that is approximately 900 meters long, taking about an hour. Along the trail, you can explore different habitats, such as aruniidud (fertile mires) and kõdusoometsad (a type of wooded swamp), and observe the local wildlife. The trail also leads to Sopa Stream and Spring, which is known as the deepest spring in Estonia.

journeys.title: Võlingi Spring Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Võlingi hiking trail's main attraction is Võlingi Spring, which is part of the Norra Springs group within the Endla Nature Reserve. The trail begins at a rest area and takes you deep into the forest until you reach a boardwalk that meanders along the edge of the spring. While hiking on this trail, you can observe the mesmerizing water display bubbling up from the spring's bottom, which is visible to the naked eye. The spring releases 220 liters of water per second. Even in winter, the spring does not freeze. The total length of the trail, out and back, is 2.4 kilometers.

journeys.title: Saarjõe Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Saarjõe hiking trail is designed for nature enthusiasts who enjoy diverse landscapes, different forest ecosystems, and cultural heritage sites. The trail closely follows the Saarjõgi River, including its ancient valley, which can be over 5 meters deep and 40-50 meters wide in some places. Along the trail, you'll also encounter the lichen-covered sand dunes known as Kabelimäed, which were formed during the Baltic Ice Lake period.
Within the trail, there is the Saeveski forest shelter, where you can rest and even spend the night under a roof. You can also have a campfire outdoors or set up a tent. The total length of the trail is approximately 8 kilometers. The trail is marked with blue signs on tree trunks.
The Saarjõe Landscape Conservation Area is home to various cultural and historical attractions, including the Sakala Road, Rassi Kabelimägi (Kabelimäed hill), bunkers used by forest brothers (resistance fighters), the Tagametsa scout camp, and the "Vanapagana" forest.
You can access the circular Saarjõe hiking trail from two points: 1. At the Metsavenna campsite, 2 kilometers from Tõrvaaugu in Järva County. 2. At the Saeveski forest shelter, 5 kilometers from Tõrvaaugu in Järva County.
Points of interest along the hiking trail provide information about charcoal production (miilimine), resin extraction (vaigutamine), various tree species, the history of Kabelimäed sand dunes, and the former inhabitants of the Saeveski forest shelter. For a leisurely nature observation and memory-preserving hike, plan on spending 4-5 hours on the trail (though it can be completed in 2-3 hours without breaks).

journeys.title: Seli Bog Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Simisalu - Matsimäe marked nature trail is 7.5 kilometers long and follows an old farm road. In parts, it was once a wooden footbridge over a swamp, filled with logs. In Kollassaare, you can still find an old farm storage building and a well. The last inhabitants left this place in the 1960s. On the path, you can observe the Ohusilla hill, which is a well-preserved ridge formed at the end of the Ice Age.
Seli Lake is a lake with a wide reed bed that's slowly becoming overgrown. Beavers build dams along the streams that flow out of the lake. From the lake's shore, the trail continues along a well-preserved slope of the bog towards the kõrgrabas (a term referring to the shape of a raised bog). The trail ends with a steep ascent to Matsimäe, which is located by Lake Pühajärv.

journeys.title: Kakerdaja Bog Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Kakerdaja Nature Trail is situated within the Kõrvemaa Landscape Protection Area and stretches for 6.2 kilometers. Beginning at the shores of Suur-Kalajärv, adorned with white water lilies, the trail takes you past the bowl-shaped Väike-Kalajärv and leads to a campfire site next to the former Noku Farm.
Heading north from the parking lot in this location, you can follow the road back to the trail's starting point at Suur-Kalajärv parking. This way, you can complete the so-called "Kalajärve Circle." As you move eastward from the Noku campfire site, a boardwalk guides hikers into the captivating, watery realm of Kakerdaja High Bog, one of the largest bogs in Kõrvemaa covering an impressive 1,000 hectares. This bog is characterized by two distinct levels or belts with hundreds of hollows that become particularly evident in the western part of the area. Both hollow systems are quite challenging to navigate. Along their edges, you can find various bog species like common cotton grass, round-leaved sundew, long-leaved sundew, and white beak-rush. The bog is also home to an Eastern Estonian species, bog asphodel.
With its brown-watered hollows and the characteristic dwarf pines that grow along their shores, Kakerdaja bog offers a picturesque view. During spring and autumn, thousands of migratory birds pause in the hollows, making it a vital resting point for them. Kakerdaja bog derives its name from the rare breeding bird species called "järvekaur" (literal translation: lake rooster), one of the many nicknames for the Eurasian curlew. While these birds are skilled divers in the water, their land movements can be rather clumsy, resembling a "kakerdamine" (clambering) motion.

journeys.title: Väätsa Hiking Trail
journeys.description: The Väätsa Nature Reserve Trail offers a compact and diverse hiking experience between the towns of Paide and Türi in Estonia. Starting at the Rõõsa Lake parking lot, the trail takes you through ancient primeval forests, where you can enjoy the beauty of lungwort flowers in the spring. It leads to the Väätsa bog, where early mornings often bring sightings of various wildlife, including deer, hares, and a variety of birds.

journeys.title: Uljaste Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Uljaste hiking trail is located in the western part of the Alutaguse plateau near Sonda and is 4.8 kilometers long. The trail begins at the parking lot near the ruins of the old Sonda forestry office, which was built in the 19th century, on the eastern shore of Lake Uljaste.
One of the prominent natural features along the trail is, of course, Lake Uljaste, which has an area of 63 hectares and a depth of 5.6 meters. The lake is surrounded by a half-curved esker (longitudinal sandy ridge) from the east, which the hiking trail mostly follows. From Tornimägi, Kiuksumägi, and Pidumägi, which are located on this esker, you can enjoy breathtaking views of Lake Uljaste and the surrounding forested areas. The lake extends to the foothills of the forested Uljaste esker both to the left (south) and to the right (west). The view from Linnamägi to the south also provides a beautiful perspective of the lake and the esker on the opposite shore.
To the west of the lake, the Uljaste bog encroaches, and within it, there are two small bog lakes: Soojärv and Saarejärv. The esker that surrounds the lake is part of an extensive chain of eskers, approximately 9 kilometers long, known as the Pohlaaru–Reinumäe–Uljaste esker chain. The Uljaste area is home to many nesting trees for the protected flying squirrel.

journeys.title: Saka Hiking Trail
journeys.description: The Saka Klindimõisa hiking trail starts on the territory of Saka Manor. The 2 km long trail leads along the cliff and the sea. On this trail, you will see the coastline, blue clay layers, coastal forests, and the Kivisilla waterfall. You can also climb to the roof of the former border guard observation tower, which offers panoramic views of the sea, and on clear days, you can even spot the Tütarsaared islands in the distance.

journeys.title: Valaste Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
The Valaste hiking and educational trail is located in the Ontika Landscape Reserve, and its total length is 1.5 km. The trail runs through the coastal forest along the edge of the cliff for about 700 meters, with a wooden footbridge on the cliff bottom and along the beach. There is a descent from the cliff of nearly 55 meters, followed by an equal ascent. Along the trail, there are 22 information boards introducing the natural and geological features of Ontika cliffs and the surrounding area.
Between the escarpment and the sea on the coastal slope, there is a coastal forest, which is remarkable for northern regions, hosting unique ecosystems and being one of the most ancient forest types in Estonia. Coastal forests provide a rich habitat for insects living in old trees and are also suitable for bats. The Valaste waterfall is also located near the trail.

journeys.title: Toila Martsa Hiking Trails
journeys.description: The Toila-Martsa hiking trail begins in the village of Toila and passes along the unique Baltic Klint, both on the high cliff edge and along the coastline. On this trail, you can experience the nature of the Baltic Klint, coastal forests, blue clay beds, granite rocks, and learn about local cultural history. The Toila ring is the shorter 3.2 km route, which offers views of the harbor, the beach, the old fishing harbor, and a German soldiers' cemetery. The longer 6.5 km trail includes the sites from the shorter route and adds blue clay beds, coastal forest, and a mill tower. The return path to Toila follows the old trade route.

journeys.title: Poruni Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Poruni hiking trail is located in Gorodenko village, Illuka municipality, within the Puhatu Nature Reserve. The trail introduces visitors to swampy forests, old broad-leaved stands, and Poruni primeval valley cliffs. The path follows the Poruni River's banks to the Narva River and back. The shorter variant is a 2.5-kilometer educational trail, which brings hikers back to the starting point in a loop, while the longer 10-kilometer trail involves walking to and from the endpoint. Information boards along the trail provide insights into the local wildlife, plants, and military history, and they also point out geological strata that are occasionally exposed along the riverbanks. There are three camping and rest areas on the educational trail. The starting and ending points feature parking and information boards.

journeys.title: Kurtna Hiking Trails
journeys.description: Kurtna hiking trail, also known as the Ten Lakes hiking trail, is located in the Kurtna landscape protection area in Illuka Parish. This 10-kilometer trail begins at the Martiska Lake's lakeside campfire site parking lot. The trail features 11 observation points equipped with information boards, and it takes about 2-2.5 hours to complete. It's possible to take a shorter route by going directly from viewpoint 6 to viewpoint 10. The trail is marked with blue markings on the trees. In wetter areas, a boardwalk makes walking easier and protects the delicate plant cover. What makes the Kurtna hiking trail unique is the dense presence of lakes in the landscape, offering hikers the opportunity to explore a wide range of lake types, experience the beauty of the local bogs, and witness the diverse mosaic of terrain and plant cover.

journeys.title: Selisoo Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Selisoo is a high bog characterized by pine forests, ridges, and well-established hummocks. The Selisoo hiking trail, one of the most fascinating bogs in Ida-Viru County, introduces various bog types (transition bog, raised bog, bog forest).
Near Seli Lake, there are lean-tos, which are open and without furnishings, where you can seek shelter from the rain or even spend the night. At the beginning and end of the trail, you can camp or have a picnic in designated areas. There is a shelter with a campfire site.
The one-way length of the boardwalk is 4 km.

journeys.title: Luite Hiking Trail
journeys.description: The hiking trail meanders in parallel to the dunes at the mouth of the Rannapungerja River and back, passing through the Kauksi camping area. The trail is marked with a yellow color stripe on the trees. The hiking trail is 4.4 km long.

journeys.title: Kauksi Oja Hiking Trail
journeys.description: The Kauksi Oja Nature Trail starts at the Alutaguse National Park Visitor Centre. The trail winds around the ancient-looking Kauksi Stream that flows into Lake Peipsi, allowing you to enjoy diverse nature and beautiful views. The 3.6 km long trail passes through forested areas where you can find plenty of blueberries during the berry season. The trail is marked with signposts and yellow markings on the trees.

journeys.title: Kotka Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Kotka hiking trail introduces low-lying bogs, transitional marshes, and sandy ridges. Notable attractions include the tall, forested sandy ridges formed during the retreat of Lake Peipsi and the enigmatic peat islands of Rüütli Bog, which were created from deposits at the end of the Ice Age. The transition from sandy ridges to the marsh is abrupt, offering hikers a pleasant change in scenery. The trail is marked with white and blue color-coded tree markings and pictograms on reddish-brown posts.

journeys.title: Endla Lake Hiking Trail
journeys.description: The Endla Lake Hiking Trail is an 8 km trail that runs between the villages of Kärde and Tooma in the Endla Nature Reserve area. You can start the trail in Kärde and finish at the Endla Nature Center in Tooma, or the other way around. This trail is meant for walking, and it is marked by yellow stripes on trees. Plan for about 3-4 hours to complete the hike. The landscape is flat, passing through forests, marshes, and along water bodies, and it includes dirt paths and boardwalks. Keep in mind that this area is a wetland, so there might be flooding during the spring.

journeys.title: Männikjärve Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Männikjärve Hiking Trail with the length of 2.2km is suitable for beginners in terms of its length. The trail offers beautiful views of Lake Männikjärv and the surrounding marsh. You have the opportunity to move around Lake Männikjärv, through the marsh, and in the mixed forest. The trail is marked with red markings on trees in the natural surroundings, and it typically takes about 1 hour to complete.

journeys.title: Männikjärve Bog Study Trail
journeys.description: The Männikjärve Bog Nature Trail is 7.3 kilometers long and starts and ends in Tooma. The trail is marked in nature with blue markings on trees. It typically takes around 2 hours to complete the trail. The trail offers an opportunity to see the entire 210-hectare Männikjärve bog from a 7-meter-high observation tower. You'll also pass numerous curving pools around the center of the bog, totaling over 120 of them in Männikjärve bog.

journeys.title: Kirna Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Kirna Nature Trail with the length of 6.8km allows you to explore the floodplains and floodplain forests along the Pedja River. There's an observation tower at Altmetsa farm, which offers a better view of the meadows and the river. In a more remote section of the trail, a suspension bridge helps you cross the river. Keep in mind that, even in drier conditions, the area may not always be dry due to the proximity of the river and its floodplains.

journeys.title: Saare Järve Study Trail
journeys.description: Kirna Nature Trail with the length of 6.8km allows you to explore the floodplains and floodplain forests along the Pedja River. There's an observation tower at Altmetsa farm, which offers a better view of the meadows and the river. In a more remote section of the trail, a suspension bridge helps you cross the river. Keep in mind that, even in drier conditions, the area may not always be dry due to the proximity of the river and its floodplains.

journeys.title: Tammeluha Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Tammeluha Nature Trail is a 2.8 km trail located between Kaiu and Jõemõisa Lakes. The trail is marked with signs and passes through a flood meadow, where a 0.6 km boardwalk has been built until Pedassaare. Please note that the boardwalk may be underwater during high water levels. This 12-point trail introduces the interesting plant life of the flood meadow and the diverse and valuable forest ecosystem of Pedassaare. The trail also features the Emamänd, the largest pine tree in the area. One of the highlights of the trail is the beautiful views of the surrounding lakes visible from Pedassaare. The lakes in this area include Jõemõisa (64 ha), Kaiu (130 ha), and Papijärve (29 ha), with Heinassaar, or Kaiu bog, located in the middle and Tammeluht as a peninsula. The Kääpa River, known from the Kalevipoeg legends, flows through this lake chain, originating from a small bog lake near the Koosa settlement and extending for 46 km.

journeys.title: Alatskivi Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Alatskivi-Lossijärve hiking trail, with its varying terrain and beautiful views around Lake Lossijärv, is a 4.5 km long trail that offers visitors plenty of experiences and attractions throughout the year.
The trail begins in Alatskivi or near the Kalevipoja säng (Kalevipoeg's Bed). Along the route, you can explore Alatskivi Castle and its park, the deer enclosure, the Oak of Fidelity, the Lamb Bridge, Kalevipoeg's Bed, Veskijärv Lake, and Lossijärv Lake.
The trail features two large information boards, a log staircase at Kalevipoeg's Bed, two bridges, signposts, and small information boards to introduce you to the specific attractions of the protected area.

journeys.title: Selli-Sillaotsa Study Trail
journeys.description: The Selli-Sillaotsa educational trail is 4.7 km long and offers a diverse hiking experience. The trail begins by passing through a pine forest along the edge of Laeva Bog, where the Suuretüki observation tower is located, providing magnificent views. The path continues with boardwalks and natural terrain sections through various stages of the bog, known for its abundant cranberry spots. The trail covers 3.5 km in the forest and bog and another 1.2 km on a gravel road.

journeys.title: Tamme Paljandi Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Tamme Paljandi Hiking Trail is located on the eastern shore of Lake Võrtsjärv, in the area between Tamme and Neemisküla. This area features several Devonian Aruküla Formation sandstone outcrops. The Tamme outcrop, found along the trail, is known as a habitat for sand martins. The sandstone of the outcrop displays various-sized holes, with larger ones being used as nesting sites by sand martins, and smaller holes created by miner bees. Additionally, there is a fascinating "guestbook" inscribed in the stone, with the oldest autograph dating back to 1936. The nearly 2 km-long trail features informational boards providing interesting insights into the local nature.

journeys.title: Mustjärve Hiking Trail
journeys.description: Mustjärve Hiking Trail is located in a boggy pine forest near Lake Võrtsjärv, offering nature enthusiasts an opportunity to experience the beauty of the natural world. This 2.3 km-long trail encircles the lake, allowing hikers to enjoy breathtaking natural views and the soothing sounds of the forest. The lake itself is large (23 hectares), with a muddy bottom and shallow waters. Its maximum depth is only 1 meter, so it's not suitable for swimming. In addition to the splendid natural surroundings, the trail has a special bonus – from the boggy forest area, you can find delicious berries like lingonberries and blueberries, which can enhance your hiking experience and allow you to savor the flavors of nature.

journeys.title: Emajõe Nature Trail
journeys.description:
The 3 km-long nature trail starts and ends at the former Kantsi tavern building and is a 1.2 m wide boardwalk that winds its way through various landscapes, including riverside meadows, marshlands with peat moss, and swampy forests. The trail includes three resting spots with camping possibilities and a floodplain observation platform. It takes about 1 hour to complete this trail.
This nature trail is part of the Peipsiveere Nature Reserve, formerly known as the Emajõe-Suursoo Nature Reserve. The main value of the Peipsiveere Nature Reserve lies in its diverse wetland habitats and the large deltaic area of the Emajõgi River.

journeys.title: Palumägede Hiking trail
journeys.description:
The 1 km-long Palumägede nature trail is located by Lake Pangodi in the Pangodi Landscape Reserve. This trail features several ascents and descents as it winds through the landscape. It begins and ends at the second parking lot of Palumäed.
The nature trail is a great way to enhance your lakeside getaway if you're camping at the Palumäed campsite and want to enjoy nature. The parking area includes a large information board, and along the trail, you'll find 8 signposts and a bench for resting.

journeys.title: Vapramäe Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Vapramäe Nature Education Trail (3.5 km) is part of the Vapramäe-Vellavere-Vitipalu network, offering a pleasant physical workout as you walk along the ridge. You can also read legends about Kalevipoeg (an Estonian mythological hero) and explore notable features like the Ancient Pine, King's Lime Tree, Kerikmägi, and much more.
This nature education trail is marked with a white-red-white symbol and starts at the Vapramäe bus stop. Along the trail, by the banks of the Elva River, you'll find a picnic area with a campfire site.

journeys.title: Elva Valley Hiking Trail
journeys.description:
Elva Primeval Valley Hiking Trail is a 15.5 km long trail with varying terrain in the Elva Landscape Reserve. It's designed for those who want to hike for multiple days. Along the trail, you'll encounter different forest landscapes, sandstone cliffs, beautiful forest lakes, the Viti Observation Tower, bogs, trenches, beavers, and the ant kingdom.
